issues.title: Initialize repo: uv init, pyproject.toml, .python-version, .gitignore
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer onboarding to this project, I want a properly initialized Python repository with uv, so that all contributors share identical dependency versions and Python runtime without manual setup.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `uv init` has been run and `pyproject.toml` exists at the repo root
- [ ] `.python-version` file contains exactly `3.12`
- [ ] Running `uv sync` completes without errors on a clean machine
- [ ] `.gitignore` excludes `.venv/`, `__pycache__/`, `*.pyc`, `.env`, and `dist/`
- [ ] `uv run python --version` prints `Python 3.12.x`
- [ ] `pyproject.toml` declares `requires-python = ">=3.12"`

## Technical Tasks
1. Install uv if not already present:
   bash
   curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh
   
2. Initialize the project:
   bash
   uv init deepresearch-agent
   cd deepresearch-agent
   
3. Pin the Python version:
   bash
   uv python pin 3.12
   # This creates .python-version containing "3.12"
   
4. Verify `pyproject.toml` looks like this (edit as needed):
   toml
   [project]
   name = "deepresearch-agent"
   version = "0.1.0"
   description = "Autonomous research agent built with LangGraph"
   requires-python = ">=3.12"
   dependencies = []
   
5. Create `.gitignore` — use GitHub's Python template as a base and ensure these entries are present:
   
   .venv/
   __pycache__/
   *.pyc
   *.pyo
   .env
   dist/
   *.egg-info/
   .mypy_cache/
   .ruff_cache/
   
6. Commit:
   bash
   git init
   git add .
   git commit -m "chore: initialize repo with uv and Python 3.12"
   

## Context
uv is a Rust-based Python package manager (think Maven/Gradle, but for Python) that replaces pip + virtualenv + pip-tools. Unlike Java where the JDK version is set globally, Python projects pin their interpreter version per-project using `.python-version`. `pyproject.toml` is the modern Python equivalent of `pom.xml` — it declares metadata, dependencies, and tool configuration in one place.

## References
- [uv Getting Started](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/getting-started/) — installation and first project walkthrough
- [uv Projects Guide](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/concepts/projects/) — explains pyproject.toml structure and uv sync
- [PEP 518 / pyproject.toml spec](https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/pyproject-toml/) — official pyproject.toml field reference

issues.title: Set up project structure (src/, tests/, docs/, configs/)
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want a well-organized source directory structure, so that code, tests, documentation, and configuration each have a clear home and Python can discover modules correctly.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Directories `src/`, `tests/`, `docs/`, and `configs/` exist at the repo root
- [ ] `src/__init__.py` exists (makes `src` a Python package)
- [ ] `src/` contains subdirectories: `agents/`, `graph/`, `tools/`, `memory/`, `prompts/`, `models/` — each with an `__init__.py`
- [ ] `tests/__init__.py` exists
- [ ] `pyproject.toml` has `[tool.setuptools.packages.find]` or equivalent so `src` is importable
- [ ] Running `uv run python -c "import src"` succeeds without error
- [ ] A placeholder `tests/test_placeholder.py` with one passing test exists

## Technical Tasks
1. Create the full directory tree:
   bash
   mkdir -p src/{agents,graph,tools,memory,prompts,models}
   mkdir -p tests docs/planning docs/adr configs
   
2. Add `__init__.py` to every Python package directory:
   bash
   touch src/__init__.py
   touch src/agents/__init__.py src/graph/__init__.py src/tools/__init__.py
   touch src/memory/__init__.py src/prompts/__init__.py src/models/__init__.py
   touch tests/__init__.py
   
3. Configure the package so uv/pip can find `src`. Add to `pyproject.toml`:
   toml
   [tool.setuptools.packages.find]
   where = ["."]
   include = ["src*"]
   
   Or, if using the src-layout convention explicitly:
   toml
   [build-system]
   requires = ["setuptools>=68"]
   build-backend = "setuptools.backends.legacy:build"
   
4. Add a placeholder test so pytest has something to collect:
   python
   # tests/test_placeholder.py
   def test_project_imports():
       import src
       assert src is not None
   
5. Verify:
   bash
   uv run python -c "import src; print('OK')"
   uv run pytest tests/test_placeholder.py -v
   
6. Commit:
   bash
   git add .
   git commit -m "chore: scaffold src/ project structure with package directories"
   

## Context
In Java, packages are enforced by the directory structure and declared in each file. Python is more flexible: a directory becomes a package only when it contains `__init__.py`. The `src/` layout (putting source code one level below the root) is a best practice that prevents accidental imports of un-installed code — similar to keeping your Java source under `src/main/java/`. The subdirectory structure maps to the architecture: `graph/` = LangGraph state machines, `tools/` = callable functions the agent can invoke, `agents/` = agent definitions for M4 multi-agent work.

## References
- [Python Packaging — src layout](https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/discussions/src-layout-vs-flat-layout/) — official guidance on src vs flat layout
- [pytest — Good Integration Practices](https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/explanation/goodpractices.html) — how pytest discovers tests and interacts with package layout

issues.title: Configure Ruff + mypy in pyproject.toml
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want Ruff and mypy configured in `pyproject.toml`, so that code style is enforced automatically and type errors are caught before runtime.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `ruff` and `mypy` are listed as dev dependencies and install via `uv sync`
- [ ] `uv run ruff check .` exits with code 0 on the current codebase
- [ ] `uv run ruff format --check .` exits with code 0
- [ ] `uv run mypy src/` exits with code 0
- [ ] Ruff is configured with `line-length = 100` and at minimum rule sets `E`, `F`, `I` (pycodestyle + pyflakes + isort)
- [ ] mypy is configured with `strict = true` and `python_version = "3.12"`
- [ ] A deliberately bad file (e.g., unused import) causes `ruff check` to fail

## Technical Tasks
1. Add dev dependencies:
   bash
   uv add --dev ruff mypy
   
2. Add Ruff configuration to `pyproject.toml`:
   toml
   [tool.ruff]
   line-length = 100
   target-version = "py312"

   [tool.ruff.lint]
   select = ["E", "F", "I", "UP", "B", "SIM"]
   # E/F = pycodestyle + pyflakes (equivalents of CheckStyle + FindBugs)
   # I   = isort (import ordering)
   # UP  = pyupgrade (modernize Python syntax)
   # B   = flake8-bugbear (common bug patterns)
   # SIM = flake8-simplify

   [tool.ruff.format]
   quote-style = "double"
   indent-style = "space"
   
3. Add mypy configuration to `pyproject.toml`:
   toml
   [tool.mypy]
   python_version = "3.12"
   strict = true
   ignore_missing_imports = true
   # strict enables: disallow_untyped_defs, warn_return_any,
   # warn_unused_ignores, etc. — comparable to Java's compiler warnings as errors
   
4. Verify both tools run cleanly:
   bash
   uv run ruff check .
   uv run ruff format .
   uv run mypy src/
   
5. Test that violations are caught — add a bad file temporarily:
   python
   # /tmp/bad.py
   import os  # unused
   x=1  # spacing violation
   
   bash
   uv run ruff check /tmp/bad.py  # should report F401 and E225
   
6. Commit:
   bash
   git add pyproject.toml uv.lock
   git commit -m "chore: configure ruff and mypy in pyproject.toml"
   

## Context
Ruff replaces three separate Java-world equivalents: CheckStyle (formatting), FindBugs/SpotBugs (bug patterns), and import organizers — all in one tool written in Rust, so it runs in milliseconds. mypy adds static type checking to Python, similar to how Java's compiler enforces types, but Python types are optional and checked separately from execution. Strict mode means every function must have type annotations — this is the right default for a production project.

## References
- [Ruff Documentation](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/) — configuration reference and rule catalog
- [Ruff Rules Index](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/) — searchable list of all lint rules and their codes
- [mypy Configuration Reference](https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/config_file.html) — all pyproject.toml options for mypy
- [mypy Strict Mode](https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/command_line.html#cmdoption-mypy-strict) — what `strict = true` enables

issues.title: Set up pre-commit hooks (ruff + mypy)
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want pre-commit hooks that run Ruff and mypy automatically before each commit, so that no linting or type errors are ever committed to the repository.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `.pre-commit-config.yaml` exists at the repo root
- [ ] `pre-commit` is listed as a dev dependency and installs via `uv sync`
- [ ] `uv run pre-commit install` has been run (hooks are registered in `.git/hooks/pre-commit`)
- [ ] Attempting to commit a file with a Ruff violation causes the commit to be rejected with an error message
- [ ] Attempting to commit a file with a mypy type error causes the commit to be rejected
- [ ] A clean commit (no violations) succeeds without manual intervention
- [ ] `uv run pre-commit run --all-files` passes on the current codebase

## Technical Tasks
1. Add pre-commit as a dev dependency:
   bash
   uv add --dev pre-commit
   
2. Create `.pre-commit-config.yaml` at the repo root:
   yaml
   repos:
     - repo: https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff-pre-commit
       rev: v0.9.0  # pin to a specific version; check for latest at the repo
       hooks:
         - id: ruff
           args: [--fix]        # auto-fix safe violations
         - id: ruff-format      # equivalent of running "ruff format"

     - repo: local
       hooks:
         - id: mypy
           name: mypy
           entry: uv run mypy src/
           language: system
           types: [python]
           pass_filenames: false  # mypy checks the whole src/ not just changed files
   
3. Install the hooks into your local git repo:
   bash
   uv run pre-commit install
   
4. Run hooks against all existing files to verify baseline passes:
   bash
   uv run pre-commit run --all-files
   
5. Test the guard rail — create a bad file and try to commit:
   bash
   echo "import os" > src/bad_temp.py
   git add src/bad_temp.py
   git commit -m "test: should be rejected"
   # Ruff should block this commit with F401 (unused import)
   rm src/bad_temp.py
   
6. Commit the config itself:
   bash
   git add .pre-commit-config.yaml pyproject.toml uv.lock
   git commit -m "chore: add pre-commit hooks for ruff and mypy"
   

## Context
Pre-commit hooks are git's built-in mechanism for running checks before a commit is recorded — conceptually similar to Maven's verify phase running before a release, except they run locally on every commit. This is the first line of defense; CI (P0-5) is the second. The `ruff-pre-commit` repo provides an optimized hook that only checks changed files, making it fast. mypy is run as a `local` hook so it uses the same uv-managed environment as the rest of the project, avoiding version mismatches.

## References
- [pre-commit Documentation](https://pre-commit.com/) — hook configuration, supported languages, and CLI reference
- [ruff-pre-commit Repository](https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff-pre-commit) — official Ruff pre-commit hook, pinned version tags

issues.title: Add GitHub Actions CI: lint, type-check, test
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want a GitHub Actions CI pipeline that runs lint, type-check, and tests on every push and pull request, so that regressions are caught automatically without relying solely on local pre-commit hooks.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `.github/workflows/ci.yml` exists and is valid YAML
- [ ] The workflow triggers on `push` to `main` and on all `pull_request` events
- [ ] CI runs on `ubuntu-latest` with Python 3.12
- [ ] The workflow has three jobs (or steps): `lint` (ruff), `type-check` (mypy), `test` (pytest)
- [ ] A passing run shows all three jobs green in the GitHub Actions UI
- [ ] Introducing a Ruff violation in a PR causes the `lint` job to fail (verified by inspection or a test PR)
- [ ] `uv sync` is used to install dependencies (not pip install)
- [ ] The workflow uses the `astral-sh/setup-uv` action for fast uv installation

## Technical Tasks
1. Create the workflow directory and file:
   bash
   mkdir -p .github/workflows
   touch .github/workflows/ci.yml
   
2. Write `.github/workflows/ci.yml`:
   yaml
   name: CI

   on:
     push:
       branches: [main]
     pull_request:

   jobs:
     lint:
       name: Lint (Ruff)
       runs-on: ubuntu-latest
       steps:
         - uses: actions/checkout@v4
         - uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v5
           with:
             python-version: "3.12"
         - run: uv sync --frozen
         - run: uv run ruff check .
         - run: uv run ruff format --check .

     type-check:
       name: Type Check (mypy)
       runs-on: ubuntu-latest
       steps:
         - uses: actions/checkout@v4
         - uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v5
           with:
             python-version: "3.12"
         - run: uv sync --frozen
         - run: uv run mypy src/

     test:
       name: Tests (pytest)
       runs-on: ubuntu-latest
       steps:
         - uses: actions/checkout@v4
         - uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v5
           with:
             python-version: "3.12"
         - run: uv sync --frozen
         - run: uv run pytest tests/ -v
   
   Note: `--frozen` ensures CI uses exactly the versions in `uv.lock`, equivalent to Maven's dependency lock file.
3. Add `pytest` and `pytest-asyncio` as dev dependencies (needed for M1+ tests, good to add now):
   bash
   uv add --dev pytest pytest-asyncio
   
4. Push to GitHub and verify the Actions tab shows the workflow running:
   bash
   git add .github/ pyproject.toml uv.lock
   git commit -m "chore: add GitHub Actions CI for lint, type-check, and test"
   git push origin main
   
5. Navigate to `https://github.com/<org>/<repo>/actions` and confirm all three jobs are green.

## Context
GitHub Actions is GitHub's built-in CI/CD system — comparable to Jenkins or GitLab CI. Each `job` runs in an isolated VM. The `astral-sh/setup-uv` action installs uv and caches the virtual environment between runs for speed, similar to how Maven's `actions/cache` caches the `.m2` repository. `uv sync --frozen` is equivalent to `mvn dependency:resolve -o` (offline, exact versions) — it ensures CI never silently upgrades a dependency. Running lint, type-check, and test as separate jobs means you see exactly which category failed without reading through interleaved output.

## References
- [GitHub Actions Documentation](https://docs.github.com/en/actions) — workflow syntax, job/step model, secrets
- [astral-sh/setup-uv Action](https://github.com/astral-sh/setup-uv) — official uv GitHub Action with caching support
- [pytest Documentation](https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/) — test collection, fixtures, and CLI flags
- [pytest-asyncio Documentation](https://pytest-asyncio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) — async test support (required for LangGraph async nodes)

issues.title: Create .env.example + configs/models.yaml (model-agnostic config)
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want a `.env.example` file and a `configs/models.yaml` for model configuration, so that API keys are never hardcoded and switching LLM providers requires only a config change, not a code change.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `.env.example` exists and documents every required environment variable with placeholder values
- [ ] `.env` is listed in `.gitignore` and is never committed
- [ ] `configs/models.yaml` exists and defines at least two model profiles (`default` and `premium`)
- [ ] A `Settings` class using `pydantic-settings` loads all env vars and raises a clear error if a required var is missing
- [ ] Running `uv run python -c "from src.models.config import Settings; print(Settings().model_dump())"` succeeds when `.env` is populated
- [ ] Unit test asserts that `Settings()` raises `ValidationError` when `TAVILY_API_KEY` is absent

## Technical Tasks
1. Add dependencies:
   bash
   uv add pydantic-settings pyyaml
   
2. Create `.env.example` (this is committed; `.env` is not):
   dotenv
   # Copy this file to .env and fill in real values. Never commit .env.

   # LLM Providers
   DEEPSEEK_API_KEY=sk-your-deepseek-key-here
   DEEPSEEK_API_KEY=your-deepseek-key-here
   DEEPSEEK_API_KEY=

   # Search
   TAVILY_API_KEY=tvly-your-key-here

   # Observability
   LANGSMITH_API_KEY=lsv2-your-key-here
   LANGSMITH_PROJECT=deepresearch-agent
   LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2=true
   
3. Create `configs/models.yaml`:
   yaml
   models:
     default:
       provider: deepseek
       model_id: deepseek-chat
       temperature: 0.7
       max_tokens: 4096
     premium:
       provider: deepseek
       model_id: deepseek-chat
       temperature: 0.5
       max_tokens: 8192
   
4. Create `src/models/config.py`:
   python
   from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings, SettingsConfigDict

   class Settings(BaseSettings):
       model_config = SettingsConfigDict(
           env_file=".env",
           env_file_encoding="utf-8",
           case_sensitive=False,
       )

       # LLM providers
       
       deepseek_api_key: str = ""
       deepseek_group_id: str = ""

       # Search
       tavily_api_key: str  # required — no default means validation fails if absent

       # Observability
       langsmith_api_key: str = ""
       langsmith_project: str = "deepresearch-agent"
       langchain_tracing_v2: bool = False

       # Active model profile
       model_profile: str = "default"

   # Singleton — import this throughout the project
   settings = Settings()
   
5. Write the unit test in `tests/test_config.py`:
   python
   import pytest
   from pydantic import ValidationError
   from src.models.config import Settings

   def test_missing_tavily_key_raises():
       with pytest.raises(ValidationError):
           Settings(tavily_api_key="")  # empty string bypasses; test without env var
   
   (Adjust test to unset the env var using `monkeypatch.delenv` for a more rigorous check.)
6. Commit:
   bash
   git add .env.example configs/models.yaml src/models/config.py tests/test_config.py
   git commit -m "chore: add env config, models.yaml, and pydantic-settings Settings class"
   

## Context
Hardcoding API keys is the Python equivalent of committing database passwords to a Java Spring `application.properties` — a serious security risk. `pydantic-settings` is the idiomatic solution: it reads from environment variables (or `.env` files) and validates them with the same Pydantic models used elsewhere in the codebase, giving you type-safe config with clear error messages. `configs/models.yaml` provides model-agnostic configuration — the code never mentions a specific model name, making provider switching a one-line YAML change.

## References
- [pydantic-settings Documentation](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/pydantic_settings/) — BaseSettings, env file loading, field validation
- [Pydantic V2 Documentation](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/) — data validation library underpinning pydantic-settings
- [PyYAML Documentation](https://pyyaml.org/wiki/PyYAMLDocumentation) — YAML parsing in Python

issues.title: Write hello-world LLM call: chat completion with DeepSeek SDK
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer learning the AI ecosystem, I want to make a working LLM API call from Python, so that I understand how to send prompts and receive completions before building agent logic on top.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A script `src/hello_llm.py` exists and runs successfully via `uv run python src/hello_llm.py`
- [ ] The script calls DeepSeek V3 via the OpenAI-compatible API
- [ ] The LLM response is printed to stdout
- [ ] API key is loaded from `.env` via the `Settings` class (not hardcoded)
- [ ] Running with an invalid API key raises a clear error (not a silent hang)
- [ ] A unit test mocks the LLM call and asserts the response is handled correctly

## Technical Tasks
1. Add LLM SDK dependencies:
   ```bash
   uv add openai  # openai SDK is used for DeepSeek's OpenAI-compatible endpoint
   ```
2. Create `src/hello_llm.py`:
   ```python
   import argparse
   from openai import OpenAI
   from src.models.config import settings

   def call_deepseek(prompt: str) -> str:
       """DeepSeek exposes an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint."""
       client = OpenAI(
           api_key=settings.deepseek_api_key,
           base_url="https://api.deepseek.com",
       )
       response = client.chat.completions.create(
           model="deepseek-chat",
           messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
           max_tokens=512,
       )
       return response.choices[0].message.content or ""

   def main() -> None:
       parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
       parser.add_argument("--prompt", default="What is LangGraph? Answer in one sentence.")
       args = parser.parse_args()

       response = call_deepseek(args.prompt)
       print(f"Response:\n{response}")

   if __name__ == "__main__":
       main()
   ```
3. Run it:
   ```bash
   uv run python src/hello_llm.py
   uv run python src/hello_llm.py --prompt "What is a ReAct agent?"
   ```
4. Write a unit test using `unittest.mock` to avoid real API calls:
   ```python
   # tests/test_hello_llm.py
   from unittest.mock import MagicMock, patch
   from src.hello_llm import call_deepseek

   def test_call_deepseek_returns_text():
       mock_response = MagicMock()
       mock_response.choices[0].message.content = "LangGraph is a library for building stateful agents."
       with patch("src.hello_llm.OpenAI") as MockClient:
           MockClient.return_value.chat.completions.create.return_value = mock_response
           result = call_deepseek("What is LangGraph?")
       assert result == "LangGraph is a library for building stateful agents."
   ```
5. Commit:
   ```bash
   git add src/hello_llm.py tests/test_hello_llm.py
   git commit -m "feat: add hello-world LLM call for DeepSeek"
   ```

## Context
This is your "Hello, World" for LLM APIs. In Java terms, think of the SDK client as a typed HTTP client (like OkHttp or Feign) — you construct a request object, call a method, and get back a typed response. The key difference from Java HTTP calls is that LLMs are non-deterministic: the same prompt can return different responses each time. DeepSeek uses an OpenAI-compatible REST API, so the OpenAI Python SDK works against it by changing only the `base_url` — this is the model-agnostic pattern you'll see throughout this project.

## References
- [DeepSeek API Documentation](https://platform.deepseek.com/api-docs) — official API docs with usage examples
- [OpenAI Python SDK](https://github.com/openai/openai-python) — used here for DeepSeek's OpenAI-compatible endpoint
- [unittest.mock — Python Docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/unittest.mock.html) — mocking library for unit tests (built into Python stdlib)

issues.title: Implement tool calling example: calculator tool with function calling
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer learning AI fundamentals, I want to implement a tool-calling example with a calculator tool, so that I understand how LLMs can invoke external functions before building the agent's real tools.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A script `src/tool_calling_example.py` exists and runs via `uv run python src/tool_calling_example.py`
- [ ] A `calculate` tool is defined and registered with the LLM
- [ ] When prompted with a math question, the LLM invokes the tool instead of guessing
- [ ] The script prints: the tool call made by the LLM, the tool result, and the final LLM response
- [ ] Tool schema is defined using a Python function with type annotations (not raw JSON)
- [ ] A unit test asserts the tool function itself returns correct results independently of the LLM

## Technical Tasks
1. No new dependencies — use the `openai` SDK already installed in P0-7.
2. Create `src/tool_calling_example.py`:
   python
   import json
   from openai import OpenAI
   from src.models.config import settings

   # Step 1: Define the tool schema
   # In Python tool calling, you describe the function in JSON Schema
   # The LLM reads this schema to know what tools are available
   CALCULATOR_TOOL = {
       "name": "calculate",
       "description": "Evaluate a basic arithmetic expression. Use this for any math.",
       "input_schema": {
           "type": "object",
           "properties": {
               "expression": {
                   "type": "string",
                   "description": "A Python arithmetic expression, e.g. '(3 + 5) * 12'",
               }
           },
           "required": ["expression"],
       },
   }

   # Step 2: Implement the tool function
   def calculate(expression: str) -> float:
       """Safely evaluate an arithmetic expression."""
       # eval() is acceptable here because this is a learning example.
       # In production, use a proper math parser.
       allowed_names: dict = {}
       return float(eval(expression, {"__builtins__": {}}, allowed_names))  # noqa: S307

   # Step 3: Agentic loop — send to LLM, handle tool call, send result back
   def run_with_tools(user_question: str) -> str:
       client = OpenAI(api_key=settings.deepseek_api_key, base_url="https://api.deepseek.com")

       messages = [{"role": "user", "content": user_question}]

       # First LLM call: may return a tool_use block
       response = client.chat.completions.create(
           model="deepseek-chat",
           max_tokens=512,
           tools=[CALCULATOR_TOOL],
           messages=messages,
       )

       print(f"Stop reason: {response.stop_reason}")

       if response.stop_reason == "tool_use":
           # Extract the tool call
           tool_use_block = next(b for b in response.content if b.type == "tool_use")
           tool_input = tool_use_block.input
           print(f"LLM called tool '{tool_use_block.name}' with: {tool_input}")

           # Execute the tool
           tool_result = calculate(tool_input["expression"])
           print(f"Tool result: {tool_result}")

           # Second LLM call: send result back
           messages += [
               {"role": "assistant", "content": response.content},
               {
                   "role": "user",
                   "content": [{
                       "type": "tool_result",
                       "tool_use_id": tool_use_block.id,
                       "content": str(tool_result),
                   }],
               },
           ]
           final = client.chat.completions.create(
               model="deepseek-chat",
               max_tokens=512,
               tools=[CALCULATOR_TOOL],
               messages=messages,
           )
           return final.content[0].text  # type: ignore[union-attr]

       return response.content[0].text  # type: ignore[union-attr]

   if __name__ == "__main__":
       answer = run_with_tools("What is (123 * 456) + 789?")
       print(f"\nFinal answer: {answer}")
   
3. Run it:
   bash
   uv run python src/tool_calling_example.py
   
   Expected output includes a tool call with expression `"123 * 456 + 789"` and the correct result `56877.0`.
4. Write a unit test for the `calculate` function (no LLM call needed):
   python
   # tests/test_tool_calling.py
   from src.tool_calling_example import calculate

   def test_calculate_basic_arithmetic():
       assert calculate("2 + 2") == 4.0

   def test_calculate_complex_expression():
       assert calculate("(123 * 456) + 789") == 56877.0
   
5. Commit:
   bash
   git add src/tool_calling_example.py tests/test_tool_calling.py
   git commit -m "feat: implement tool calling example with calculator tool"
   

## Context
Tool calling (also called function calling) is the mechanism by which LLMs can interact with the outside world — instead of hallucinating an answer, the LLM says "I need to call this function" and your code executes it. In Java terms, think of it as a remote procedure call where the LLM is the caller and your Python function is the callee, with JSON Schema as the interface definition (like a WSDL). This two-turn pattern (send prompt → receive tool call → send result → receive final answer) is the foundation of every agent in this project.

## References
- [DeepSeek Tool Use (OpenAI-compatible format)](https://platform.deepseek.com/api-docs/zh-cn/function-calling) — complete guide to defining tools, the request/response cycle, and multi-turn tool use
- [DeepSeek/OpenAI Tool Use API Reference](https://platform.deepseek.com/api-docs) — `tool_use` and `tool_result` content block schemas
- [JSON Schema Reference](https://json-schema.org/understanding-json-schema/) — how to write `input_schema` for tool definitions

issues.title: Integrate Tavily search: query → results → LLM summary
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want to integrate Tavily search so the agent can retrieve real web results and summarize them with an LLM, so that I understand the full search-to-answer pipeline before it is formalized as a LangGraph tool.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `tavily-python` is added as a project dependency
- [ ] A script `src/tavily_example.py` runs via `uv run python src/tavily_example.py "your query"`
- [ ] The script returns at least 3 Tavily search results for the given query
- [ ] Search results (title, URL, snippet) are printed before the LLM summary
- [ ] An LLM summarizes the results into a 2–3 sentence answer with source URLs cited
- [ ] `TAVILY_API_KEY` is loaded from `.env` via `Settings` (not hardcoded)
- [ ] A unit test mocks the Tavily client and asserts the results-formatting logic works correctly

## Technical Tasks
1. Add the Tavily SDK:
   bash
   uv add tavily-python
   
2. Create `src/tavily_example.py`:
   python
   import sys
   from openai import OpenAI
   from tavily import TavilyClient
   from src.models.config import settings

   def search(query: str, max_results: int = 5) -> list[dict]:
       """Query Tavily and return structured results."""
       client = TavilyClient(api_key=settings.tavily_api_key)
       response = client.search(
           query=query,
           search_depth="basic",   # "basic" uses fewer API credits; "advanced" is deeper
           max_results=max_results,
           include_answer=False,   # We'll use the LLM for summarization instead
       )
       return response["results"]  # list of {title, url, content, score}

   def format_results_for_llm(results: list[dict]) -> str:
       """Convert Tavily results into a text block the LLM can reason over."""
       lines = []
       for i, r in enumerate(results, start=1):
           lines.append(f"[{i}] {r['title']}")
           lines.append(f"    URL: {r['url']}")
           lines.append(f"    {r['content'][:400]}...")
           lines.append("")
       return "\n".join(lines)

   def summarize_with_llm(query: str, formatted_results: str) -> str:
       """Ask the LLM to produce a cited summary of the search results."""
       client = OpenAI(api_key=settings.deepseek_api_key, base_url="https://api.deepseek.com")
       prompt = (
           f"Based on the following search results for the query '{query}', "
           "write a 2-3 sentence summary and cite sources by their [number].\n\n"
           f"{formatted_results}"
       )
       response = client.chat.completions.create(
           model="deepseek-chat",
           max_tokens=512,
           messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
       )
       return response.content[0].text  # type: ignore[union-attr]

   def main() -> None:
       query = " ".join(sys.argv[1:]) if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "What is LangGraph?"
       print(f"Searching for: {query}\n")

       results = search(query)
       formatted = format_results_for_llm(results)

       print("=== Search Results ===")
       print(formatted)

       print("=== LLM Summary ===")
       summary = summarize_with_llm(query, formatted)
       print(summary)

   if __name__ == "__main__":
       main()
   
3. Run it:
   bash
   uv run python src/tavily_example.py "LangGraph stateful agents"
   
4. Write a unit test that mocks Tavily and tests `format_results_for_llm`:
   python
   # tests/test_tavily_example.py
   from src.tavily_example import format_results_for_llm

   MOCK_RESULTS = [
       {"title": "LangGraph Intro", "url": "https://example.com/1", "content": "LangGraph is a library..."},
       {"title": "LangGraph Tutorial", "url": "https://example.com/2", "content": "In this tutorial..."},
   ]

   def test_format_results_contains_urls():
       formatted = format_results_for_llm(MOCK_RESULTS)
       assert "https://example.com/1" in formatted
       assert "https://example.com/2" in formatted
       assert "[1]" in formatted
       assert "[2]" in formatted
   
5. Commit:
   bash
   git add src/tavily_example.py tests/test_tavily_example.py pyproject.toml uv.lock
   git commit -m "feat: integrate Tavily search with LLM summarization"
   

## Context
This script is the manual prototype of what will become the agent's `web_search` tool in M1. By building it as a standalone script first, you can experiment with Tavily's API parameters and understand the result schema before LangGraph wraps it. Tavily is purpose-built for LLM applications — unlike raw Google search results, Tavily returns cleaned, chunked text optimized for fitting into an LLM context window. The pattern here (search → format → summarize) maps directly to the Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pattern you will use with ChromaDB in M3.

## References
- [Tavily Python SDK](https://docs.tavily.com/sdk/python) — client initialization, search parameters, response schema
- [Tavily API Reference](https://docs.tavily.com/api-reference/endpoint/search) — full list of search parameters (`search_depth`, `max_results`, `include_answer`, etc.)
- [Tavily Quickstart](https://docs.tavily.com/welcome) — API key setup and first search

issues.title: Configure LangSmith tracing, verify traces visible in dashboard
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want LangSmith tracing configured and verified, so that every LLM call made by the agent is recorded in the LangSmith dashboard for debugging and performance analysis.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `langsmith` package is installed as a project dependency
- [ ] LangSmith environment variables are documented in `.env.example` (`LANGSMITH_API_KEY`, `LANGSMITH_PROJECT`, `LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2=true`)
- [ ] Running `src/hello_llm.py` (from P0-7) with tracing enabled produces a visible trace in the LangSmith dashboard at `smith.langchain.com`
- [ ] The trace shows the model name, input prompt, output, token counts, and latency
- [ ] A `src/tracing.py` module exists that configures tracing and exports a `get_tracer()` helper
- [ ] Running with `LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2=false` disables tracing and produces no LangSmith entries

## Technical Tasks
1. Add the LangSmith and LangChain core packages:
   bash
   uv add langsmith langchain-core langchain-openai
   
   Note: `langchain-openai` wraps the LLM SDK in LangChain's `ChatModel` interface, which automatically enables tracing.
2. Update `.env.example` with the required vars (if not already done in P0-6):
   dotenv
   LANGSMITH_API_KEY=lsv2-your-key-here
   LANGSMITH_PROJECT=deepresearch-agent
   LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2=true
   LANGCHAIN_ENDPOINT=https://api.smith.langchain.com
   
3. Create `src/tracing.py`:
   python
   import os
   from langsmith import Client
   from src.models.config import settings

   def configure_tracing() -> None:
       """Set environment variables that enable LangChain auto-tracing."""
       os.environ["LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2"] = str(settings.langchain_tracing_v2).lower()
       os.environ["LANGCHAIN_PROJECT"] = settings.langsmith_project
       if settings.langsmith_api_key:
           os.environ["LANGSMITH_API_KEY"] = settings.langsmith_api_key

   def get_client() -> Client:
       """Return a LangSmith client for manual run logging if needed."""
       return Client(api_key=settings.langsmith_api_key)
   
4. Update `src/hello_llm.py` to use `langchain-openai` so calls are auto-traced:
   python
   # Use LangChain's wrapper for auto-tracing
   from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
   from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage
   from src.tracing import configure_tracing

   configure_tracing()  # call before any LLM invocation

   def call_deepseek_traced(prompt: str) -> str:
       llm = ChatOpenAI(
           model="deepseek-chat",
           api_key=settings.deepseek_api_key,
           max_tokens=512,
       )
       response = llm.invoke([HumanMessage(content=prompt)])
       return str(response.content)
   
5. Run and verify:
   bash
   uv run python src/hello_llm.py --profile default
   # Then open https://smith.langchain.com → your project → check for a new trace
   
6. Verify tracing is off when the flag is false:
   bash
   LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2=false uv run python src/hello_llm.py --profile default
   # No new trace should appear in LangSmith
   
7. Commit:
   bash
   git add src/tracing.py pyproject.toml uv.lock
   git commit -m "feat: configure LangSmith tracing with langchain-openai wrapper"
   

## Context
LangSmith is the observability platform for LangChain/LangGraph applications — think of it as the Datadog or Jaeger for AI agents. When `LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2=true`, every LLM call made through any LangChain component is automatically recorded without changing your code. This is invaluable for debugging agent behavior: you can replay exactly what the LLM saw, what it returned, how many tokens were used, and where latency came from. In M1 and beyond, every LangGraph node execution will appear as a nested span in these traces. The `langchain-openai` wrapper is the first step toward the model-agnostic design described in CLAUDE.md — provider switching becomes a config change because all models implement the same `ChatModel` interface.

## References
- [LangSmith Documentation](https://docs.smith.langchain.com/) — setup, tracing concepts, dashboard navigation
- [LangSmith Quickstart](https://docs.smith.langchain.com/observability/quickstart) — environment variable setup and first trace verification
- [langchain-openai PyPI / Docs](https://python.langchain.com/docs/integrations/chat/openai/) — ChatOpenAI usage and configuration
- [LangChain ChatModel Interface](https://python.langchain.com/docs/concepts/chat_models/) — the common interface all LangChain LLM wrappers implement

issues.title: Write README: project vision, tech stack, setup instructions
issues.description:
## User Story
As a new contributor or evaluator, I want a clear README at the repo root, so that I can understand the project's purpose, set up my environment, and run the agent within 15 minutes of cloning the repo.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `README.md` exists at the repo root
- [ ] The README includes: project description, tech stack table, prerequisites, setup instructions, and usage examples
- [ ] Following the setup instructions on a clean machine (or fresh Python environment) results in a working installation
- [ ] All commands in the README are copy-pasteable and correct (tested manually)
- [ ] The README links to `docs/planning/project_background.md` for deeper context
- [ ] A "Current Milestone" section indicates which milestone is complete and what is in progress

## Technical Tasks
1. Create `README.md` at the repo root with the following sections:

   **Project title and one-line description**
   markdown
   # DeepResearch Agent
   An autonomous research agent that searches the web, analyzes information,
   and produces structured research reports with citations.
   Built with LangGraph + Python 3.12.
   

   **Tech Stack table**
   markdown
   | Layer | Technology |
   |---|---|
   | Agent framework | LangGraph (LangChain) |
   | Language | Python 3.12 |
   | Package manager | uv |
   | Lint / Format | Ruff |
   | Type checker | mypy |
   | Testing | pytest |
   | LLM (default) | DeepSeek V3 |
   | LLM (premium) | DeepSeek V3 |
   | Search | Tavily |
   | Observability | LangSmith |
   

   **Prerequisites**
   - uv installed (`curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh`)
   - Python 3.12 (uv manages this automatically)
   - API keys for DeepSeek, Tavily, and LangSmith

   **Setup instructions** (exact commands, in order):
   bash
   git clone <repo-url>
   cd deepresearch-agent
   uv sync
   cp .env.example .env
   # Edit .env and add your API keys
   uv run pre-commit install
   

   **Verification**:
   bash
   uv run python src/hello_llm.py          # test LLM call
   uv run python src/tavily_example.py "LangGraph"  # test search
   uv run pytest                            # run all tests
   

   **Development commands**:
   bash
   uv run ruff check .       # lint
   uv run ruff format .      # format
   uv run mypy src/          # type check
   

   **Milestone status table**:
   markdown
   | Milestone | Description | Status |
   |---|---|---|
   | P0 | Scaffolding, tooling, hello-world | Complete |
   | M1 | ReAct agent + CLI | In Progress |
   | M2 | FastAPI + planning sub-graphs | Planned |
   | M3 | Memory + RAG | Planned |
   | M4 | Multi-agent collaboration | Planned |
   | M5 | Next.js web UI + deployment | Planned |
   

   **Further reading**: link to `docs/planning/project_background.md`

2. Proof-read every command by running it in a shell.
3. Commit:
   bash
   git add README.md
   git commit -m "docs: add project README with setup instructions and milestone status"
   

## Context
A README is the front door of a software project. For a Java developer learning Python, clear setup instructions are especially important because the toolchain is different: there is no IDE-based project import — everything is command-line driven. The README should be opinionated and prescriptive: one correct way to set up, one correct way to run. Avoid listing options or alternatives in the README; those belong in `docs/`.

## References
No new technologies are introduced in this issue.
