issues.title: Spike: LangGraph quickstart + LangChain Academy ReAct tutorial
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer new to LangGraph, I want to complete the official LangGraph quickstart and LangChain Academy ReAct tutorial, so that I understand the core abstractions (StateGraph, nodes, edges, tools) before writing production code.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] LangGraph quickstart notebook runs end-to-end locally with no errors
- [ ] LangChain Academy Module 1 (Agent Architectures) completed; certificate or completion screenshot saved to `docs/til/`
- [ ] TIL note written at `docs/til/langgraph-react-concepts.md` covering: StateGraph, nodes, edges, checkpointing, and the ReAct loop
- [ ] Developer can explain the difference between a LangGraph `node` and an `edge` in their own words (captured in TIL)
- [ ] Java concept mappings documented: StateGraph ↔ Spring State Machine, node ↔ @Service method, tool ↔ @Component bean

## Technical Tasks
1. Install LangGraph in the project environment:
   bash
   uv add langgraph langchain-core
   uv sync
   
2. Follow the LangGraph quickstart (linked below). Run each cell; observe how `StateGraph`, `add_node`, `add_edge`, and `compile()` work together.
3. Enroll in LangChain Academy (free). Complete "Introduction to LangGraph" — focus on Module 1 (Simple Graph) and Module 2 (ReAct Agent).
4. Write your TIL note. Template:
   markdown
   # TIL: LangGraph + ReAct Concepts
   ## Key abstractions
   - StateGraph: ...
   - Node: ...
   - Edge / Conditional Edge: ...
   ## ReAct loop
   reason → act → observe → reason ...
   ## Java analogies
   - StateGraph ≈ Spring State Machine
   - Node ≈ @Service method
   - Tool ≈ @Component bean injected by the agent
   
5. Commit the TIL note: `git commit -m "docs: TIL langgraph and ReAct concepts (M1-1)"`

## Context
This spike is the foundation for every subsequent M1 issue. LangGraph is the orchestration layer — it defines how the agent reasons, calls tools, and loops. Without understanding StateGraph and the ReAct pattern, the code in M1-5 will be opaque.

ReAct (Reason + Act) is a prompting/agent pattern where the LLM alternates between "thinking" (reasoning trace) and "acting" (tool calls), then observes the result and loops. This is analogous to a state machine: the LLM is the transition function, tools are side-effecting transitions, and the graph defines legal state changes.

## References
- [LangGraph Quickstart](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/tutorials/introduction/) — official getting-started tutorial; run this first
- [LangChain Academy – Introduction to LangGraph](https://academy.langchain.com/courses/intro-to-langgraph) — free course; complete Modules 1–2
- [LangGraph Concepts: Nodes, Edges, State](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/concepts/low_level/) — reference for core abstractions
- [ReAct Paper (arXiv)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.03629) — original academic paper describing the ReAct pattern

issues.title: Implement web_search tool (Tavily API wrapper, configurable)
issues.description:
## User Story
As the research agent, I want a `web_search` tool that queries Tavily and returns structured results, so that I can discover relevant sources for any research query.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `src/tools/web_search.py` exists and defines a function decorated with `@tool`
- [ ] Tool accepts a `query: str` parameter and an optional `max_results: int` (default: 5)
- [ ] Tool returns a list of dicts, each with keys: `title`, `url`, `snippet`, `score`
- [ ] Tavily API key is read from the environment variable `TAVILY_API_KEY` (never hardcoded)
- [ ] `uv run python -c "from src.tools.web_search import web_search; import asyncio; print(asyncio.run(web_search.ainvoke({'query': 'LangGraph tutorial'})))"` returns results without error
- [ ] `uv run pytest tests/tools/test_web_search.py` passes (unit test mocks Tavily HTTP call)
- [ ] `uv run mypy src/tools/web_search.py` passes with no errors

## Technical Tasks
1. Add dependencies:
   bash
   uv add langchain-tavily python-dotenv
   uv sync
   
2. Add `TAVILY_API_KEY=tvly-...` to your `.env` file (obtain key at https://app.tavily.com).
3. Create `src/tools/web_search.py`:
   python
   from langchain_core.tools import tool
   from langchain_tavily import TavilySearch
   from typing import Any

   @tool
   async def web_search(query: str, max_results: int = 5) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:
       """Search the web for information about a topic.

       Args:
           query: The search query string.
           max_results: Maximum number of results to return (default 5).

       Returns:
           List of results with title, url, snippet, and relevance score.
       """
       client = TavilySearch(max_results=max_results)
       response = await client.ainvoke(query)
       return [
           {
               "title": r.get("title", ""),
               "url": r.get("url", ""),
               "snippet": r.get("content", ""),
               "score": r.get("score", 0.0),
           }
           for r in response.get("results", [])
       ]
   
4. Write the unit test at `tests/tools/test_web_search.py` using `unittest.mock.patch` to mock `TavilySearch.ainvoke` — do not make real HTTP calls in tests.
5. Verify types: `uv run mypy src/tools/web_search.py`
6. Verify formatting: `uv run ruff check src/tools/web_search.py && uv run ruff format src/tools/web_search.py`

## Context
`web_search` is the agent's primary discovery mechanism — it answers "what URLs are relevant to this query?" It intentionally does not fetch full page content (that is `web_fetch`, M1-3). Keeping tools single-responsibility makes them independently testable and swappable.

In Java terms, `@tool` is analogous to a Spring `@Bean` that gets registered in an application context — except here the "context" is the LangGraph agent, and the LLM decides when to invoke it.

## References
- [LangChain `@tool` decorator](https://python.langchain.com/docs/concepts/tools/) — how to define tools LangGraph agents can call
- [langchain-tavily package](https://python.langchain.com/docs/integrations/tools/tavily_search/) — official Tavily integration docs
- [Tavily API dashboard](https://app.tavily.com) — obtain your API key here

issues.title: Implement web_fetch tool (httpx + trafilatura, content extraction)
issues.description:
## User Story As the research agent, I want a `web_fetch` tool that downloads a URL and extracts its readable text content, so that I can analyze the full body of a source page rather than just a search snippet. ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] `src/tools/web_fetch.py` exists and defines an async `@tool` named `web_fetch` - [ ] Tool accepts a `url: str` parameter and returns a `dict` with keys: `url`, `title`, `content`, `word_count` - [ ] HTTP requests use `httpx.AsyncClient` with a configurable timeout (default: 10 s) - [ ] Content extraction uses `trafilatura.extract()` — raw HTML is never returned to the agent - [ ] Returns a structured error dict (not an exception) when fetch fails: `{"url": url, "error": "", "content": ""}` - [ ] `uv run pytest tests/tools/test_web_fetch.py` passes (uses `httpx` mock / `respx` for HTTP mocking) - [ ] `uv run mypy src/tools/web_fetch.py` passes with no errors - [ ] Pages longer than 8,000 words are truncated to 8,000 words before returning ## Technical Tasks 1. Add dependencies: bash uv add httpx trafilatura respx uv sync 2. Create `src/tools/web_fetch.py`: python import httpx import trafilatura from langchain_core.tools import tool from typing import Any MAX_WORDS = 8_000 TIMEOUT_SECONDS = 10 @tool async def web_fetch(url: str) -> dict[str, Any]: """Fetch a web page and extract its readable text content. Args: url: The full URL to fetch (must include http:// or https://). Returns: Dict with url, title, content (plain text), and word_count. On failure, returns a dict with url and error keys. """ try: async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=TIMEOUT_SECONDS) as client: response = await client.get(url, follow_redirects=True) response.raise_for_status() html = response.text except httpx.HTTPError as e: return {"url": url, "error": str(e), "content": "", "title": "", "word_count": 0} content = trafilatura.extract(html, include_comments=False, include_tables=False) or "" title = trafilatura.extract_metadata(html).title if content else "" words = content.split() if len(words) > MAX_WORDS: content = " ".join(words[:MAX_WORDS]) return { "url": url, "title": title or "", "content": content, "word_count": min(len(words), MAX_WORDS), } 3. Write tests in `tests/tools/test_web_fetch.py`. Use `respx` to mock HTTP responses: python import respx, httpx, pytest from src.tools.web_fetch import web_fetch @pytest.mark.asyncio async def test_web_fetch_extracts_content(): with respx.mock: respx.get("https://example.com").mock( return_value=httpx.Response(200, text="

Hello world

") ) result = await web_fetch.ainvoke({"url": "https://example.com"}) assert result["content"] != "" assert result["word_count"] > 0 4. Test the truncation path: mock a response whose extracted text exceeds 8,000 words. 5. Test the error path: mock a 404 or connection error and assert the returned dict has an `error` key. ## Context `web_fetch` answers "what does this page actually say?" — complementing `web_search` which finds URLs. Together they form the agent's full research pipeline: discover → fetch → analyze. Using `trafilatura` instead of raw HTML parsing means the agent gets clean article text, not nav menus, cookie banners, or JavaScript. Truncating at 8,000 words prevents LLM context overflow — a constraint that has no Java equivalent but is a core concern in LLM engineering. httpx is the Python async equivalent of Java's `WebClient` (from Spring WebFlux). `async with` is analogous to try-with-resources. ## References - [httpx documentation](https://www.python-httpx.org/) — async HTTP client; see "Async" section - [trafilatura documentation](https://trafilatura.readthedocs.io/) — web content extraction; see `extract()` and metadata functions - [respx – httpx mocking](https://lundberg.github.io/respx/) — mock HTTP calls in pytest; analogous to WireMock in Java - [LangChain `@tool` decorator](https://python.langchain.com/docs/concepts/tools/) — tool definition reference

issues.title: Implement note_take tool (save findings to local scratchpad)
issues.description:
## User Story
As the research agent, I want a `note_take` tool that appends findings to an in-memory scratchpad in the agent state, so that I can accumulate and reference information across multiple reasoning steps without losing context.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `src/tools/note_take.py` exists and defines a `@tool` named `note_take`
- [ ] Tool accepts `note: str` and appends it to a `notes` list in LangGraph agent state
- [ ] Notes persist across tool calls within a single agent run (verified by integration test)
- [ ] `src/models/state.py` defines a `ResearchState` TypedDict that includes a `notes: list[str]` field
- [ ] `uv run pytest tests/tools/test_note_take.py` passes
- [ ] `uv run mypy src/tools/note_take.py src/models/state.py` passes with no errors

## Technical Tasks
1. Define the shared agent state model in `src/models/state.py`:
   python
   from typing import Annotated
   from langgraph.graph import add_messages
   from langchain_core.messages import BaseMessage
   import operator

   class ResearchState(TypedDict):
       messages: Annotated[list[BaseMessage], add_messages]  # chat history
       notes: Annotated[list[str], operator.add]             # accumulated findings
       query: str                                            # original research question
   
   Note: `Annotated[list[str], operator.add]` tells LangGraph to *append* new notes rather than overwrite the list — this is LangGraph's reducer pattern.
2. Create `src/tools/note_take.py`:
   python
   from langchain_core.tools import tool

   @tool
   def note_take(note: str) -> str:
       """Save an important finding or insight to the research scratchpad.

       Use this tool to record key facts, quotes, or conclusions as you
       research. Notes are accumulated and available for the final report.

       Args:
           note: The finding or insight to save.

       Returns:
           Confirmation string.
       """
       # The actual state mutation happens via the graph's state reducer.
       # The tool returns a confirmation; LangGraph injects notes into state
       # via the ToolMessage → node pattern (see M1-5).
       return f"Note saved: {note}"
   
   > Note: In M1-5 you will wire this so the tool's output is captured and appended to `state["notes"]` inside the agent node.
3. Write `tests/tools/test_note_take.py` — test that invoking `note_take.ainvoke({"note": "foo"})` returns the expected confirmation string.
4. Run: `uv run ruff format src/tools/note_take.py src/models/state.py`

## Context
LangGraph's state is immutable between nodes — each node returns a *delta* that gets merged with the current state via reducer functions. `operator.add` on a list means "append new items." This is analogous to an immutable value object pattern in Java (like a record being replaced by a new instance), but managed automatically by the framework.

The scratchpad pattern is important for multi-step research: the agent needs to remember what it has already found so it does not re-fetch the same information or contradict itself in the final report.

## References
- [LangGraph State and Reducers](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/concepts/low_level/#state) — explains the `Annotated` reducer pattern
- [LangGraph State Schema](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/how-tos/state-model/) — how to define TypedDict state with custom reducers
- [LangChain `@tool` decorator](https://python.langchain.com/docs/concepts/tools/) — tool definition reference

issues.title: Build ReAct agent graph: reason → act → observe loop
issues.description:
## User Story
As a user submitting a research query, I want the agent to autonomously reason about what to search, call tools, observe results, and loop until it has gathered enough information to produce an answer, so that I receive a well-researched response without manual intervention.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `src/graph/agent.py` defines a compiled LangGraph `StateGraph` using `ResearchState`
- [ ] Graph has at minimum three nodes: `reason` (LLM call), `act` (tool execution), `observe` (result injection)
- [ ] Conditional edge routes from `reason` to either `act` (if tool call requested) or `END` (if final answer)
- [ ] All three tools (`web_search`, `web_fetch`, `note_take`) are bound to the agent LLM
- [ ] Agent correctly terminates after producing a final answer (no infinite loop)
- [ ] `uv run python -m src.cli --query "What is LangGraph?"` runs end-to-end and prints a non-empty answer
- [ ] `uv run pytest tests/graph/test_agent.py` passes using a mocked LLM (no real API calls)
- [ ] `uv run mypy src/graph/agent.py` passes with no errors
- [ ] LangSmith trace visible in dashboard showing full reason→act→observe loop

## Technical Tasks
1. Add LangSmith tracing env vars to `.env`:
   
   LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2=true
   LANGCHAIN_API_KEY=ls__...
   LANGCHAIN_PROJECT=deepresearch-m1
   
2. Create `src/graph/agent.py` — the core graph:
   python
   from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
   from langgraph.prebuilt import ToolNode
   from langchain_core.messages import AIMessage
   from src.models.state import ResearchState
   from src.tools.web_search import web_search
   from src.tools.web_fetch import web_fetch
   from src.tools.note_take import note_take
   from src.graph.nodes import reason_node, should_continue

   tools = [web_search, web_fetch, note_take]

   def build_graph(llm):
       tool_node = ToolNode(tools)
       llm_with_tools = llm.bind_tools(tools)

       graph = StateGraph(ResearchState)
       graph.add_node("reason", reason_node(llm_with_tools))
       graph.add_node("act", tool_node)

       graph.set_entry_point("reason")
       graph.add_conditional_edges(
           "reason",
           should_continue,
           {"continue": "act", "end": END},
       )
       graph.add_edge("act", "reason")  # observe → reason (loop back)

       return graph.compile()
   
3. Create `src/graph/nodes.py` with `reason_node` and `should_continue`:
   python
   from langchain_core.messages import AIMessage
   from src.models.state import ResearchState

   def reason_node(llm):
       async def _node(state: ResearchState):
           response = await llm.ainvoke(state["messages"])
           return {"messages": [response]}
       return _node

   def should_continue(state: ResearchState) -> str:
       last_message = state["messages"][-1]
       if isinstance(last_message, AIMessage) and last_message.tool_calls:
           return "continue"
       return "end"
   
4. `ToolNode` (from `langgraph.prebuilt`) handles tool dispatch automatically — it reads `tool_calls` from the last `AIMessage`, invokes the matching tool, and returns a `ToolMessage`. You do not need to write this dispatch logic manually.
5. Write the integration test in `tests/graph/test_agent.py` using `FakeListLLM` or `FakeToolCallingModel` (see M1-10 for details). Simulate one tool call followed by a final answer.
6. Verify the full graph structure with: `graph.get_graph().print_ascii()`

## Context
This is the architectural core of the entire project. Everything else (M2 planning, M3 memory, M4 multi-agent) is built on top of this ReAct loop.

The pattern maps to Java like this:
- `StateGraph` ≈ Spring State Machine with a typed state context
- `reason_node` ≈ a `@Service` that calls an external API (the LLM) and returns a delta
- `ToolNode` ≈ a `@Component` dispatcher that routes method calls by name (like reflection-based dispatch)
- `conditional_edges` ≈ guard conditions on state machine transitions
- `END` ≈ a terminal state

The loop terminates when the LLM produces an `AIMessage` with no `tool_calls` — the LLM itself decides when it has enough information. This is fundamentally different from traditional Java control flow where termination is explicit.

## References
- [LangGraph StateGraph API](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/reference/graphs/) — complete graph builder reference
- [LangGraph ToolNode (prebuilt)](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/reference/prebuilt/#langgraph.prebuilt.tool_node.ToolNode) — prebuilt tool execution node
- [LangGraph Conditional Edges](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/concepts/low_level/#conditional-edges) — routing based on state
- [LangSmith Tracing Setup](https://docs.smith.langchain.com/observability/how_to_guides/tracing/trace_with_langgraph) — enable tracing with env vars
- [ReAct Agent How-To](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/how-tos/create-react-agent/) — official LangGraph ReAct guide

issues.title: Implement model router: load provider/model from configs/models.yaml
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want all LLM provider and model configuration loaded from `configs/models.yaml` at startup, so that I can switch between providers without changing any Python code.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `configs/models.yaml` exists and specifies at minimum: provider, model name, temperature, and max tokens
- [ ] `src/models/config.py` defines a Pydantic model `ModelConfig` that validates the YAML schema
- [ ] `src/graph/factory.py` exposes a `create_llm(config_path)` factory function that returns a `BaseChatModel`
- [ ] Current default is DeepSeek; additional providers can be added later via config
- [ ] `uv run pytest tests/test_factory.py` passes — tests that the default provider returns a `BaseChatModel` instance
- [ ] `uv run mypy src/models/config.py src/graph/factory.py` passes with no errors
- [ ] API keys for each provider are read from environment variables (never from YAML)

## Technical Tasks
1. Add dependencies:
   ```bash
   uv add pyyaml pydantic langchain-openai
   uv sync
   ```
   DeepSeek: use `langchain-openai` with a custom `base_url` (DeepSeek is OpenAI-compatible).
2. Define `configs/models.yaml`:
   ```yaml
   default_provider: deepseek

   providers:
     deepseek:
       model: deepseek-chat
       base_url: https://api.deepseek.com
       api_key_env: DEEPSEEK_API_KEY
       temperature: 0.7
       max_tokens: 4096
     # Additional providers (anthropic, openai, etc.) can be added here
     # when model switching feature is implemented
   ```
3. Define `src/models/config.py` using Pydantic v2:
   ```python
   from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
   import yaml, os

   class ProviderConfig(BaseModel):
       model: str
       api_key_env: str
       temperature: float = 0.7
       max_tokens: int = 4096
       base_url: str | None = None

   class ModelsConfig(BaseModel):
       default_provider: str
       providers: dict[str, ProviderConfig]

   def load_config(path: str = "configs/models.yaml") -> ModelsConfig:
       with open(path) as f:
           data = yaml.safe_load(f)
       return ModelsConfig.model_validate(data)
   ```
4. Define `src/graph/factory.py`:
   ```python
   import os
   from langchain_core.language_models import BaseChatModel
   from src.models.config import ModelsConfig, load_config

   def create_llm(config_path: str = "configs/models.yaml", provider: str | None = None) -> BaseChatModel:
       config = load_config(config_path)
       provider_name = provider or config.default_provider
       pc = config.providers[provider_name]
       api_key = os.environ[pc.api_key_env]

       # All current providers use OpenAI-compatible API format
       from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
       kwargs = {"base_url": pc.base_url} if pc.base_url else {}
       return ChatOpenAI(model=pc.model, api_key=api_key,
                         temperature=pc.temperature, max_tokens=pc.max_tokens, **kwargs)
   ```
5. Write `tests/test_factory.py` — mock `os.environ` and assert `create_llm()` returns a `BaseChatModel` for the default provider.

## Context
This is the dependency-injection layer for the LLM. In Java/Spring, you'd define a `@Bean` for each provider and select via `@Profile` or `@ConditionalOnProperty`. Here, the factory function plays that role, with YAML as the application.properties equivalent.

Keeping API keys in environment variables (not YAML) is a 12-factor app principle — the same principle as using Spring `@Value("${api.key}")` rather than hardcoding credentials.

Currently only DeepSeek is configured. The model-agnostic architecture (via `BaseChatModel` interface) means adding other providers later is a config + factory change, not a refactor.

## References
- [LangChain Chat Models concept](https://python.langchain.com/docs/concepts/chat_models/) — `BaseChatModel` interface and provider list
- [langchain-openai](https://python.langchain.com/docs/integrations/chat/openai/) — `ChatOpenAI` usage (also covers OpenAI-compatible APIs like DeepSeek)
- [Pydantic v2 BaseModel](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/models/) — model validation and `model_validate()`
- [PyYAML documentation](https://pyyaml.org/wiki/PyYAMLDocumentation) — `yaml.safe_load()` reference

issues.title: Add streaming output (token + tool call events)
issues.description:
## User Story
As a user running a research query, I want to see the agent's reasoning tokens and tool calls streamed to the terminal in real time, so that I can follow progress during long-running queries rather than waiting for a final answer.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `src/graph/streaming.py` exposes an `async def stream_agent(graph, inputs)` function that uses `astream_events`
- [ ] Token chunks are printed to stdout as they arrive (not buffered until completion)
- [ ] Tool call events print a line such as `[tool] web_search(query="LangGraph")` before the tool executes
- [ ] Tool result events print a summary line such as `[result] web_search → 5 results`
- [ ] Final answer is clearly delimited (e.g., prefixed with `\n=== Answer ===\n`)
- [ ] `uv run python -m src.cli --query "What is LangGraph?"` streams output visibly (manually verified)
- [ ] Streaming function handles `asyncio.CancelledError` gracefully (Ctrl+C exits cleanly)
- [ ] `uv run mypy src/graph/streaming.py` passes with no errors

## Technical Tasks
1. No new dependencies required — `astream_events` is part of `langchain-core`.
2. Create `src/graph/streaming.py`:
   python
   import asyncio
   from typing import Any
   from langgraph.graph.state import CompiledStateGraph

   async def stream_agent(graph: CompiledStateGraph, inputs: dict[str, Any]) -> None:
       """Stream agent events to stdout in real time."""
       try:
           async for event in graph.astream_events(inputs, version="v2"):
               kind = event["event"]

               if kind == "on_chat_model_stream":
                   chunk = event["data"]["chunk"]
                   if chunk.content:
                       print(chunk.content, end="", flush=True)

               elif kind == "on_tool_start":
                   name = event["name"]
                   tool_input = event["data"].get("input", {})
                   print(f"\n[tool] {name}({tool_input})", flush=True)

               elif kind == "on_tool_end":
                   name = event["name"]
                   output = event["data"].get("output", "")
                   summary = str(output)[:120]  # truncate long outputs
                   print(f"[result] {name} → {summary}", flush=True)

       except asyncio.CancelledError:
           print("\n[interrupted]")
           raise

       print("\n=== Answer ===")
   
3. Update `src/cli.py` to call `stream_agent` instead of `graph.ainvoke`.
4. Manually test with a real query: `uv run python -m src.cli --query "What is LangGraph?"`
   - You should see tokens appear word-by-word, tool calls logged inline, and the answer delimited at the end.
5. Verify types: `uv run mypy src/graph/streaming.py`

## Context
Streaming is critical UX for an agent that may take 30–60 seconds on a multi-step research task. Without streaming, the terminal appears frozen and users cannot tell if the agent is working.

`astream_events` is LangGraph's event-based streaming API — it emits fine-grained events (`on_chat_model_stream`, `on_tool_start`, `on_tool_end`, etc.) as the graph executes. This is analogous to reactive/event streams in Java (Project Reactor `Flux`), except consumed with Python's `async for`.

`version="v2"` is required — v1 is deprecated as of LangChain 0.2.

## References
- [LangGraph Streaming How-To](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/how-tos/streaming/) — complete streaming guide with event type reference
- [astream_events API](https://python.langchain.com/docs/concepts/streaming/#astream_events) — event schema for `on_chat_model_stream`, `on_tool_start`, `on_tool_end`
- [Python asyncio — CancelledError](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-exceptions.html#asyncio.CancelledError) — handling Ctrl+C in async code

issues.title: Build CLI with Rich: single-shot + interactive mode
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer or researcher, I want a polished CLI that supports both single-shot queries (`--query "..."`) and an interactive REPL mode, so that I can use the agent efficiently from the terminal without writing Python code.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `src/cli.py` is the entry point, runnable via `uv run python -m src.cli`
- [ ] `--query "<question>"` flag runs a single query and exits
- [ ] `--interactive` flag (or `-i`) starts a REPL loop; `exit` or `quit` or Ctrl+D exits cleanly
- [ ] `--provider <name>` flag overrides the default provider from `configs/models.yaml`
- [ ] Rich is used for formatted output: spinner during agent run, colored tool call events, markdown-rendered final answer
- [ ] `uv run python -m src.cli --help` prints usage with all flags documented
- [ ] `uv run pytest tests/test_cli.py` passes (uses `click.testing.CliRunner` or `typer.testing.CliRunner`)
- [ ] `uv run mypy src/cli.py` passes with no errors

## Technical Tasks
1. Add dependencies:
   bash
   uv add typer rich
   uv sync
   
2. Create `src/cli.py`:
   python
   import asyncio
   import typer
   from rich.console import Console
   from rich.markdown import Markdown
   from rich.status import Status
   from src.graph.agent import build_graph
   from src.graph.factory import create_llm
   from src.graph.streaming import stream_agent
   from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage

   app = typer.Typer(help="DeepResearch Agent CLI")
   console = Console()

   def _run_query(query: str, provider: str | None) -> None:
       llm = create_llm(provider=provider)
       graph = build_graph(llm)
       inputs = {"messages": [HumanMessage(content=query)], "notes": [], "query": query}
       asyncio.run(stream_agent(graph, inputs))

   @app.command()
   def main(
       query: str = typer.Option(None, "--query", "-q", help="Single research query"),
       interactive: bool = typer.Option(False, "--interactive", "-i", help="Start interactive REPL"),
       provider: str = typer.Option(None, "--provider", "-p", help="LLM provider override"),
   ):
       if query:
           _run_query(query, provider)
       elif interactive:
           console.print("[bold green]DeepResearch Agent[/] — type your question, or 'exit' to quit.")
           while True:
               try:
                   q = console.input("[bold blue]>[/] ").strip()
               except (EOFError, KeyboardInterrupt):
                   break
               if q.lower() in ("exit", "quit", ""):
                   break
               _run_query(q, provider)
       else:
           console.print("[red]Provide --query or --interactive[/]")
           raise typer.Exit(1)

   if __name__ == "__main__":
       app()
   
3. Add a Rich `Status` spinner around the `asyncio.run(stream_agent(...))` call so users see a spinner before the first token arrives.
4. Render the final answer with `console.print(Markdown(answer_text))` for formatted output.
5. Write `tests/test_cli.py` using `typer.testing.CliRunner`:
   python
   from typer.testing import CliRunner
   from src.cli import app
   from unittest.mock import patch

   runner = CliRunner()

   def test_missing_args_exits_nonzero():
       result = runner.invoke(app, [])
       assert result.exit_code == 1

   def test_help():
       result = runner.invoke(app, ["--help"])
       assert result.exit_code == 0
       assert "--query" in result.output
   

## Context
Typer is the Python equivalent of Picocli (Java). It uses type annotations to define CLI arguments — no boilerplate `argparse` configuration. Rich is a terminal rendering library with no direct Java equivalent; it handles colors, spinners, tables, and Markdown rendering in the terminal.

The interactive REPL mode is important for developer productivity: run the agent multiple times in a session without reloading the process (and paying the Python import startup cost each time).

## References
- [Typer documentation](https://typer.tiangolo.com/) — CLI framework; start with "First Steps"
- [Rich documentation](https://rich.readthedocs.io/) — terminal formatting; see Console, Status (spinner), Markdown
- [Typer testing](https://typer.tiangolo.com/tutorial/testing/) — `CliRunner` for unit-testing CLI commands

issues.title: Add error handling: tool failures, API timeouts, retry logic
issues.description:
## User Story
As the research agent, I want tool failures and API timeouts to be retried with exponential backoff and logged with structured context, so that transient network errors do not abort a research session.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `web_search` and `web_fetch` tools retry up to 3 times on `httpx.TimeoutException` and HTTP 5xx errors, with exponential backoff
- [ ] After 3 failed attempts, a structured error dict is returned to the agent (no uncaught exception)
- [ ] All retry attempts are logged using `structlog` at `WARNING` level with fields: `tool`, `attempt`, `error`
- [ ] Successful tool calls are logged at `DEBUG` level with `tool` and `duration_ms` fields
- [ ] `src/utils/logging.py` configures structlog once at application startup
- [ ] `uv run pytest tests/test_retry.py` passes — tests assert retry count and final error return
- [ ] `uv run mypy src/utils/logging.py` passes with no errors

## Technical Tasks
1. Add dependencies:
   bash
   uv add structlog tenacity
   uv sync
   
2. Create `src/utils/logging.py`:
   python
   import structlog

   def configure_logging(log_level: str = "INFO") -> None:
       structlog.configure(
           processors=[
               structlog.stdlib.add_log_level,
               structlog.stdlib.add_logger_name,
               structlog.dev.ConsoleRenderer(),  # human-readable in dev
           ],
           wrapper_class=structlog.make_filtering_bound_logger(
               __import__("logging").getLevelName(log_level)
           ),
           context_class=dict,
           logger_factory=structlog.PrintLoggerFactory(),
       )

   logger = structlog.get_logger()
   
3. Add retry logic to `web_search` using `tenacity`:
   python
   from tenacity import retry, stop_after_attempt, wait_exponential, retry_if_exception_type
   from src.utils.logging import logger
   import httpx, time

   @retry(
       stop=stop_after_attempt(3),
       wait=wait_exponential(multiplier=1, min=1, max=10),
       retry=retry_if_exception_type((httpx.TimeoutException, httpx.HTTPStatusError)),
       reraise=False,
   )
   async def _fetch_with_retry(url: str) -> dict:
       ...
   
   Wrap the internal HTTP call — not the `@tool` function itself — so the tool always returns a value to the agent.
4. Log retries using a `tenacity` `before_sleep` callback:
   python
   from tenacity import before_sleep_log
   import logging
   # or use structlog directly in a custom before_sleep callback
   
5. Apply the same pattern to `web_fetch`.
6. Call `configure_logging()` in `src/cli.py` at startup.
7. Write `tests/test_retry.py` — use `unittest.mock` to make the inner function raise `httpx.TimeoutException` twice then succeed; assert the tool returns a valid result and that the mock was called 3 times.

## Context
In production, search APIs and web servers are unreliable. Without retry logic, a single timeout aborts the entire research session. `tenacity` provides exponential backoff decorators — analogous to Spring Retry's `@Retryable(maxAttempts=3, backoff=@Backoff(delay=1000, multiplier=2))`.

`structlog` replaces Python's standard `logging` module with structured, context-rich log records. Instead of `logger.warning("Retry attempt 2")`, you write `logger.warning("tool_retry", tool="web_search", attempt=2, error=str(e))` — producing machine-parseable JSON logs in production. This is analogous to SLF4J + Logback's MDC pattern in Java.

## References
- [tenacity documentation](https://tenacity.readthedocs.io/) — retry library; see `stop_after_attempt`, `wait_exponential`, `retry_if_exception_type`
- [structlog documentation](https://www.structlog.org/en/stable/) — structured logging; see "Getting Started" and "Configuration"
- [httpx Exceptions](https://www.python-httpx.org/exceptions/) — exception hierarchy for retry targeting

issues.title: Write integration tests with FakeListLLM mock
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want integration tests for the ReAct agent graph that run without real API calls, so that I can verify agent behavior in CI without incurring cost or network dependencies.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `tests/graph/test_agent.py` contains at least 3 integration test cases using a fake LLM
- [ ] Test 1: agent calls `web_search` once then produces a final answer (single-tool loop)
- [ ] Test 2: agent calls `web_search` then `web_fetch` then produces a final answer (multi-tool loop)
- [ ] Test 3: agent produces a final answer immediately without calling any tool
- [ ] No test makes real HTTP calls or real LLM API calls
- [ ] `uv run pytest tests/graph/test_agent.py -v` passes with all tests green
- [ ] Tests run in under 5 seconds total (no real network I/O)
- [ ] `pytest-asyncio` mode is set to `asyncio_mode = "auto"` in `pyproject.toml`

## Technical Tasks
1. Add dependencies:
   bash
   uv add pytest-asyncio
   uv sync
   
2. Configure pytest-asyncio in `pyproject.toml`:
   toml
   [tool.pytest.ini_options]
   asyncio_mode = "auto"
   
3. Use `langchain_core.language_models.fake.FakeListLLM` for simple scripted responses, or `langchain_core.language_models.fake.GenericFakeChatModel` / `FakeToolCallingModel` for tool-call simulation:
   python
   from langchain_core.messages import AIMessage, ToolCall

   def make_fake_llm_with_tool_call(tool_name: str, tool_args: dict, final_answer: str):
       """Returns a fake LLM that first makes one tool call, then gives a final answer."""
       from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, MagicMock
       llm = MagicMock()
       tool_call_msg = AIMessage(
           content="",
           tool_calls=[ToolCall(name=tool_name, args=tool_args, id="tc1")]
       )
       final_msg = AIMessage(content=final_answer)
       llm.ainvoke = AsyncMock(side_effect=[tool_call_msg, final_msg])
       llm.bind_tools = MagicMock(return_value=llm)
       return llm
   
4. Mock the tool functions themselves to avoid HTTP calls:
   python
   from unittest.mock import patch, AsyncMock

   @pytest.mark.asyncio
   async def test_single_tool_loop():
       fake_llm = make_fake_llm_with_tool_call(
           "web_search", {"query": "LangGraph"}, "LangGraph is a framework."
       )
       with patch("src.tools.web_search.web_search", new_callable=AsyncMock) as mock_search:
           mock_search.return_value = [{"title": "LangGraph", "url": "https://example.com",
                                        "snippet": "...", "score": 0.9}]
           graph = build_graph(fake_llm)
           result = await graph.ainvoke({
               "messages": [HumanMessage(content="What is LangGraph?")],
               "notes": [],
               "query": "What is LangGraph?",
           })
       assert "LangGraph" in result["messages"][-1].content
   
5. Repeat the pattern for Test 2 (chained tool calls) and Test 3 (no tool call).
6. Run the full test suite: `uv run pytest -v`

## Context
In Java/Spring, you'd use Mockito to mock service dependencies in unit tests. Here, `unittest.mock.AsyncMock` plays the same role for async Python functions. `pytest-asyncio` is the equivalent of JUnit's async test support (similar to `@Test` with a reactive test runner).

The key insight is that `FakeListLLM` / mocked LLMs let you script the exact sequence of LLM responses, so you can test the graph's routing logic (does it route to `act` when there's a tool call? does it route to `END` when there isn't?) independently of real model behavior.

## References
- [pytest-asyncio documentation](https://pytest-asyncio.readthedocs.io/) — async test runner; see `asyncio_mode = "auto"` configuration
- [LangChain fake LLMs](https://python.langchain.com/docs/concepts/testing/) — `FakeListLLM`, `GenericFakeChatModel` for test scripting
- [unittest.mock.AsyncMock](https://docs.python.org/3/library/unittest.mock.html#asyncmock) — mocking async functions in Python
- [LangGraph Testing Guide](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/concepts/application_structure/#testing) — recommended patterns for testing LangGraph applications

issues.title: ADR-001: Why LangGraph, agent loop design decisions
issues.description:
## User Story
As a future maintainer of this project, I want an Architecture Decision Record explaining why LangGraph was chosen and how the ReAct agent loop is designed, so that I can understand the reasoning behind the architecture without having to re-research the alternatives.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `docs/adr/ADR-001-langgraph-agent-loop.md` exists and follows the MADR template in `docs/adr/template.md`
- [ ] ADR documents at least 2 considered alternatives to LangGraph (e.g., LlamaIndex Workflows, raw LangChain AgentExecutor, custom loop)
- [ ] ADR explains the ReAct loop design: why conditional edges over fixed loops, why `ToolNode` over custom dispatch
- [ ] ADR includes a Mermaid diagram of the agent graph
- [ ] ADR status is set to "Accepted"
- [ ] `git log --oneline` shows a commit with message `docs: ADR-001 LangGraph agent loop design (M1-11)`

## Technical Tasks
1. Read the existing template: `docs/adr/template.md`
2. Create `docs/adr/ADR-001-langgraph-agent-loop.md` following the MADR format. Key sections to complete:

   **Status:** Accepted

   **Context:** We need an agent orchestration framework that supports: (1) cyclic graphs (ReAct requires loops), (2) typed state shared across nodes, (3) streaming of intermediate steps, (4) first-class tool use.

   **Decision:** Use LangGraph's `StateGraph` with a `reason → act` loop and conditional edges for termination detection.

   **Alternatives Considered:**
   - *LangChain AgentExecutor (legacy):* Does not support custom graph topology; hard to extend with planning sub-graphs (needed at M2).
   - *LlamaIndex Workflows:* Event-driven rather than graph-based; less native integration with LangChain tool ecosystem.
   - *Custom asyncio loop:* Maximum flexibility but requires reimplementing checkpointing, streaming, and state management from scratch.

   **Consequences:** LangGraph lock-in for orchestration; benefits are first-class streaming, checkpointing (M3), and multi-agent patterns (M4) with minimal extra code.

3. Include a Mermaid diagram:
   `markdown
   mermaid
   graph TD
       START([START]) --> reason[reason\nLLM call]
       reason -->|tool_calls present| act[act\nToolNode]
       reason -->|no tool_calls| END([END])
       act --> reason
   
   `
4. Commit: `git commit -m "docs: ADR-001 LangGraph agent loop design (M1-11)"`

## Context
ADRs (Architecture Decision Records) are lightweight documents that capture *why* a technology or pattern was chosen, not just *what* was chosen. They are especially valuable when onboarding new team members or revisiting decisions 6 months later.

MADR (Markdown Any Decision Records) is a specific ADR format that is widely adopted in open-source projects. It is analogous to a Java project's `ARCHITECTURE.md` or Confluence design doc, but version-controlled alongside the code.

## References
- [MADR format specification](https://adr.github.io/madr/) — the template standard this project follows
- [ADR GitHub organization](https://adr.github.io/) — background on ADRs and when to write them
- [Mermaid diagram syntax](https://mermaid.js.org/syntax/flowchart.html) — flowchart syntax for the agent graph diagram
