issues.title: Spike: LangGraph persistence, checkpointer API, RAG patterns
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want to understand LangGraph persistence, checkpointer APIs, and RAG patterns before implementation, so that I can make informed architectural decisions and avoid costly rewrites.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Written spike notes summarizing how LangGraph checkpointers work (thread_id, checkpoint_id, what gets serialized)
- [ ] Written notes comparing at least 2 embedding models (OpenAI text-embedding-3-small vs sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2) — cost, latency, quality trade-offs
- [ ] Written notes describing the RAG retrieval loop: embed query → vector similarity search → inject context into prompt
- [ ] ChromaDB quick-start script runs locally and stores/retrieves at least 3 documents
- [ ] Spike findings committed to `docs/til/m3-memory-spike.md`

## Technical Tasks
1. Read the LangGraph persistence conceptual guide (linked below). Key concepts to understand: a `checkpointer` is a backend (SQLite, Postgres, Redis) that saves the entire graph `State` after every node execution, keyed by `thread_id`. Think of it as Java's `HttpSession` but for agent state.
2. Run the LangGraph checkpointer quick-start in a scratch script:
   python
   from langgraph.checkpoint.sqlite import SqliteSaver
   # More detail in M3-2 — just understand the API shape here
   
3. Read the ChromaDB quick-start guide. Install and run a local in-process instance:
   bash
   uv add chromadb
   python - <<'EOF'
   import chromadb
   client = chromadb.Client()
   col = client.create_collection("spike")
   col.add(documents=["LangGraph is a graph-based agent framework"], ids=["doc1"])
   results = col.query(query_texts=["agent framework"], n_results=1)
   print(results)
   EOF
   
4. Read the LangChain RAG conceptual overview. Map it to a Java analogy: RAG is like a database lookup before calling a service — instead of SQL, you embed the query into a vector and find the nearest neighbors.
5. Compare embedding models: note API cost for OpenAI (paid per token) vs sentence-transformers (free, local, slower to load). Record your recommendation.
6. Write up findings in `docs/til/m3-memory-spike.md`. Use headings: Checkpointer API, RAG Pattern, ChromaDB, Embedding Models, Open Questions.

## Context
M3 adds two forms of memory to the agent: (1) **conversation persistence** — the agent remembers what it said earlier in a session, and (2) **semantic memory** — the agent can recall research it performed in past sessions by similarity. Without this spike, implementation risks poor embedding model choices and misuse of the checkpointer API. This is the only pure learning task in M3; all other issues build on its findings.

## References
- [LangGraph Persistence Conceptual Guide](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/concepts/persistence/) — explains thread_id, checkpointers, and what state is saved
- [LangGraph Checkpointers How-To](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/how-tos/persistence/) — working code examples
- [ChromaDB Quick-Start](https://docs.trychroma.com/getting-started) — install, create collection, add and query documents
- [LangChain RAG Conceptual Overview](https://python.langchain.com/docs/concepts/rag/) — retrieval-augmented generation explained
- [OpenAI Embeddings Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/embeddings) — model names, dimensions, pricing
- [sentence-transformers Documentation](https://www.sbert.net/docs/quickstart.html) — local embedding models, no API key required

issues.title: Add LangGraph SQLite checkpointer for conversation state
issues.description:
## User Story
As a user, I want my conversation state to persist across CLI sessions using a SQLite checkpointer, so that the agent remembers the current research thread and I do not have to repeat context.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `uv add langgraph-checkpoint-sqlite` is recorded in `pyproject.toml` / `uv.lock`
- [ ] A `thread_id` is accepted as a CLI argument (e.g. `--thread-id my-research-1`); defaults to a new UUID if omitted
- [ ] Agent state is persisted to `data/checkpoints.sqlite` after each graph execution
- [ ] Running the CLI twice with the same `--thread-id` restores prior state (verified by checking that the agent's message history is non-empty on the second run)
- [ ] Running with a new `--thread-id` starts a clean session
- [ ] Unit test `tests/test_checkpointer.py` asserts that two sequential runs with the same thread_id share state

## Technical Tasks
1. Install the SQLite checkpointer backend:
   bash
   uv add langgraph-checkpoint-sqlite
   
2. Create `src/memory/checkpointer.py`. Initialize the checkpointer and expose a `get_checkpointer()` factory:
   python
   from pathlib import Path
   from langgraph.checkpoint.sqlite import SqliteSaver

   DB_PATH = Path("data/checkpoints.sqlite")

   def get_checkpointer() -> SqliteSaver:
       DB_PATH.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
       return SqliteSaver.from_conn_string(str(DB_PATH))
   
3. Pass the checkpointer when compiling the LangGraph graph in `src/graph/builder.py`:
   python
   from src.memory.checkpointer import get_checkpointer

   checkpointer = get_checkpointer()
   app = graph.compile(checkpointer=checkpointer)
   
4. Update `src/cli.py` to accept and forward `thread_id`:
   python
   import click, uuid

   @click.option("--thread-id", default=None, help="Resume a previous session")
   def main(query: str, thread_id: str | None):
       tid = thread_id or str(uuid.uuid4())
       config = {"configurable": {"thread_id": tid}}
       result = app.invoke({"query": query}, config=config)
   
   Think of `thread_id` as a Java `HttpSession` ID — it namespaces all saved state for one conversation.
5. Add `data/` to `.gitignore` so the SQLite file is never committed.
6. Write `tests/test_checkpointer.py`: invoke the graph twice with the same thread_id and assert the second invocation's state contains messages from the first.

## Context
Without persistence, every CLI invocation starts from scratch. The LangGraph checkpointer saves the entire `State` dataclass after every node in the graph, keyed by `(thread_id, checkpoint_id)`. This is the foundation for incremental research (M3-7): the agent needs to know what it already found before deciding what to search next. SQLite is used here for zero-infrastructure simplicity; production deployment would swap to the Postgres checkpointer without changing application code.

## References
- [LangGraph SQLite Checkpointer (langgraph-checkpoint-sqlite)](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/reference/checkpoints/#langgraph.checkpoint.sqlite.SqliteSaver) — API reference for `SqliteSaver`
- [LangGraph Persistence How-To](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/how-tos/persistence/) — end-to-end example with thread_id and config
- [LangGraph Checkpoints Concept](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/concepts/persistence/) — explains what is checkpointed and when

issues.title: Set up ChromaDB: collection creation, embedding pipeline
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want ChromaDB set up with a working embedding pipeline, so that research findings can be stored and retrieved by semantic similarity in subsequent tasks.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `chromadb` and `langchain-chroma` are added to `pyproject.toml`
- [ ] `src/memory/vector_store.py` exists and exposes a `get_collection(name: str)` function
- [ ] Running `uv run python -m src.memory.vector_store` (as `__main__`) adds 3 test documents and retrieves the most relevant one — output is printed and correct
- [ ] Embedding model is driven by config in `configs/models.yaml` (not hardcoded)
- [ ] Unit test `tests/test_vector_store.py` passes: adds documents, queries, asserts at least 1 result is returned
- [ ] `data/chroma/` is in `.gitignore`

## Technical Tasks
1. Install dependencies:
   bash
   uv add chromadb langchain-chroma
   # For local embeddings (no API key needed during development):
   uv add sentence-transformers
   
2. Add embedding model config to `configs/models.yaml`:
   yaml
   embeddings:
     provider: sentence-transformers   # or openai
     model: all-MiniLM-L6-v2           # fast, 384-dim, free
     # model: text-embedding-3-small   # use when provider: openai
   
3. Create `src/memory/vector_store.py`:
   python
   import chromadb
   from chromadb.config import Settings
   from langchain_chroma import Chroma
   from langchain_community.embeddings import SentenceTransformerEmbeddings
   from pathlib import Path

   CHROMA_PATH = Path("data/chroma")

   def get_embedding_function():
       # Read from configs/models.yaml in M3-10; hardcode for now
       return SentenceTransformerEmbeddings(model_name="all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

   def get_collection(name: str = "research") -> Chroma:
       CHROMA_PATH.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
       return Chroma(
           collection_name=name,
           embedding_function=get_embedding_function(),
           persist_directory=str(CHROMA_PATH),
       )
   
   Think of `Chroma` as a Java `EntityManager` for vectors — it handles serialization, storage, and query translation.
4. Add a `__main__` block for smoke-testing:
   python
   if __name__ == "__main__":
       col = get_collection("smoke_test")
       col.add_texts(
           texts=["LangGraph is a graph framework", "ChromaDB stores vectors", "Python is dynamic"],
           ids=["d1", "d2", "d3"],
       )
       results = col.similarity_search("vector database", k=1)
       print(results)
   
5. Add `data/chroma/` to `.gitignore`.
6. Write `tests/test_vector_store.py` using a temporary directory for `persist_directory` so tests do not pollute `data/chroma/`.

## Context
ChromaDB is the vector store that enables semantic memory. Unlike a relational database (which finds exact matches), ChromaDB finds documents whose *meaning* is closest to a query by comparing embedding vectors using cosine similarity. The embedding model converts text → a list of floats (e.g. 384 numbers for all-MiniLM-L6-v2). This is the storage layer that `memory_store` (M3-4) and `memory_recall` (M3-5) will write and read.

## References
- [ChromaDB Python Quick-Start](https://docs.trychroma.com/getting-started) — client modes, collections, add/query API
- [langchain-chroma Integration Docs](https://python.langchain.com/docs/integrations/vectorstores/chroma/) — LangChain wrapper API, add_texts, similarity_search
- [sentence-transformers Pre-trained Models](https://www.sbert.net/docs/sentence_transformer/pretrained_models.html) — model list, dimensions, speed benchmarks
- [ChromaDB Embeddings Guide](https://docs.trychroma.com/docs/embeddings/embedding-functions) — how to plug in custom embedding functions

issues.title: Implement memory_store tool: save research findings as embeddings
issues.description:
## User Story
As a researcher agent, I want a `memory_store` tool that saves research findings as embeddings in ChromaDB, so that I can recall relevant past findings in future sessions without re-searching the web.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `src/tools/memory_store.py` exists and exports a LangChain `@tool`-decorated function `memory_store`
- [ ] The tool accepts `content: str` and optional `metadata: dict` (topic, source URL, timestamp)
- [ ] Long content is chunked into segments ≤ 512 tokens before embedding (verified by unit test with a 2000-word input)
- [ ] Each chunk is stored in the `research` ChromaDB collection with a unique ID and metadata
- [ ] The tool returns a confirmation string: `"Stored N chunks from [source]."`
- [ ] The agent graph in `src/graph/builder.py` includes `memory_store` in its tool list
- [ ] Unit test `tests/test_memory_store.py` passes: stores a document and asserts the collection `count()` increased

## Technical Tasks
1. Install a text splitter if not already available (it ships with `langchain-text-splitters`):
   bash
   uv add langchain-text-splitters
   
2. Create `src/tools/memory_store.py`:
   python
   from langchain_core.tools import tool
   from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
   from src.memory.vector_store import get_collection
   import hashlib, time

   _splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
       chunk_size=512,
       chunk_overlap=64,
       length_function=len,  # swap for tiktoken in M3-10
   )

   @tool
   def memory_store(content: str, source: str = "unknown", topic: str = "") -> str:
       """Save research findings to long-term memory for future recall."""
       chunks = _splitter.split_text(content)
       collection = get_collection("research")
       ids = [hashlib.md5(f"{source}-{i}".encode()).hexdigest() for i in range(len(chunks))]
       metadatas = [{"source": source, "topic": topic, "timestamp": int(time.time())} for _ in chunks]
       collection.add_texts(texts=chunks, ids=ids, metadatas=metadatas)
       return f"Stored {len(chunks)} chunks from {source}."
   
   Text chunking is analogous to paginating a large Java object before persisting it — it keeps individual records within size limits and improves retrieval precision.
3. Register the tool in `src/graph/builder.py`:
   python
   from src.tools.memory_store import memory_store
   tools = [...existing tools..., memory_store]
   
4. Write `tests/test_memory_store.py`. Use a `tmp_path` fixture to point ChromaDB at a temp directory so tests are isolated:
   python
   def test_memory_store_chunks(tmp_path, monkeypatch):
       monkeypatch.setenv("CHROMA_PATH", str(tmp_path))
       result = memory_store.invoke({"content": "word " * 600, "source": "test"})
       assert "Stored" in result
       assert int(result.split()[1]) > 1  # must have chunked
   

## Context
This tool gives the agent **write access** to its long-term memory. Chunking is essential because embedding models have token limits and also because smaller, focused chunks return more precise similarity matches than large blobs. The `metadata` dict (source URL, topic, timestamp) enables filtered recall in M3-5 and pruning in M3-8. This follows the same `@tool` pattern used in M1 for `web_search` and `web_fetch`.

## References
- [LangChain @tool Decorator](https://python.langchain.com/docs/how_to/custom_tools/) — how to create tools from functions
- [LangChain RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter](https://python.langchain.com/docs/how_to/recursive_text_splitter/) — chunk_size, chunk_overlap, length_function options
- [langchain-chroma add_texts API](https://python.langchain.com/docs/integrations/vectorstores/chroma/) — ids, metadatas parameters

issues.title: Implement memory_recall tool: retrieve relevant past findings
issues.description:
## User Story
As a researcher agent, I want a `memory_recall` tool that retrieves the most relevant past research findings by semantic similarity, so that I can avoid re-researching topics and build on prior work.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `src/tools/memory_recall.py` exists and exports a LangChain `@tool`-decorated function `memory_recall`
- [ ] The tool accepts `query: str` and optional `k: int` (default 5) and `topic_filter: str | None`
- [ ] Results are returned as a formatted string: each result shows its source, topic, and content snippet
- [ ] When `topic_filter` is provided, only chunks whose metadata `topic` field matches are returned
- [ ] When ChromaDB is empty the tool returns `"No relevant memory found."` without raising an exception
- [ ] The agent graph in `src/graph/builder.py` includes `memory_recall` in its tool list
- [ ] Unit test `tests/test_memory_recall.py` passes: stores 3 documents with different topics, queries for one topic, asserts only relevant results are returned

## Technical Tasks
1. Create `src/tools/memory_recall.py`:
   python
   from langchain_core.tools import tool
   from src.memory.vector_store import get_collection

   @tool
   def memory_recall(query: str, k: int = 5, topic_filter: str | None = None) -> str:
       """Retrieve relevant past research findings from long-term memory."""
       collection = get_collection("research")
       if collection._collection.count() == 0:
           return "No relevant memory found."

       where = {"topic": topic_filter} if topic_filter else None
       results = collection.similarity_search_with_score(
           query=query, k=k, filter=where
       )
       if not results:
           return "No relevant memory found."

       lines = []
       for doc, score in results:
           lines.append(
               f"[score={score:.2f}] source={doc.metadata.get('source','?')} "
               f"topic={doc.metadata.get('topic','?')}\n{doc.page_content[:300]}"
           )
       return "\n\n".join(lines)
   
   `similarity_search_with_score` is analogous to a SQL `ORDER BY relevance_score DESC LIMIT k` — except the distance metric is cosine similarity over embedding vectors.
2. Register the tool in `src/graph/builder.py`:
   python
   from src.tools.memory_recall import memory_recall
   tools = [...existing tools..., memory_recall]
   
3. Write `tests/test_memory_recall.py`:
   - Use `monkeypatch` / `tmp_path` to isolate the ChromaDB path
   - Store documents with topics `"climate"`, `"economy"`, `"climate"`
   - Query `memory_recall.invoke({"query": "greenhouse gases", "topic_filter": "climate"})` and assert `"economy"` does not appear in the result
   - Assert the empty-collection case returns the no-memory string
4. Tune the score threshold if needed: results with `score > 1.5` (L2 distance) are likely irrelevant. Add a `MIN_SCORE` constant and filter them out.

## Context
This tool gives the agent **read access** to its long-term memory. Together with `memory_store` (M3-4), it closes the RAG loop: store findings after research, recall them before planning the next search. The `topic_filter` enables the planner (M3-6) to check only memory relevant to the current research topic, reducing noise. The score threshold prevents low-quality matches from polluting the context window.

## References
- [ChromaDB Query / Filtering Docs](https://docs.trychroma.com/docs/querying-collections/query-and-get) — `where` metadata filters, `n_results`
- [langchain-chroma similarity_search_with_score](https://python.langchain.com/docs/integrations/vectorstores/chroma/) — returns (Document, float) tuples
- [LangChain @tool Decorator](https://python.langchain.com/docs/how_to/custom_tools/) — tool schema generation from type hints

issues.title: Integrate memory into planner: skip known topics, focus on gaps
issues.description:
## User Story
As a research planner, I want to query long-term memory before generating a research plan, so that I skip topics already covered and focus the agent's effort on genuine knowledge gaps.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The planner node in `src/graph/nodes/planner.py` calls `memory_recall` before generating sub-questions
- [ ] The planner prompt includes a `{memory_context}` section that is populated with recalled findings (or `"No prior memory."` if empty)
- [ ] In a test where ChromaDB contains a document about topic X, a new query about topic X produces a plan that references prior findings rather than re-searching from scratch (verified by inspecting the plan string in a unit test)
- [ ] When memory is empty the planner behaves identically to its pre-M3 behavior (regression test passes)
- [ ] LangSmith trace shows the `memory_recall` tool call as a node in the graph trace

## Technical Tasks
1. Update the planner system prompt in `src/prompts/planner.py` (or equivalent) to include a memory section:
   python
   PLANNER_PROMPT = """
   You are a research planner. Before generating sub-questions, review what is already known:

   ## Prior Research Memory
   {memory_context}

   ## Instructions
   - If the memory already covers a sub-topic well, do NOT include it as a new search task.
   - Focus new search tasks on gaps not covered by memory.
   - Generate 3-5 focused sub-questions.

   ## Query
   {query}
   """
   
2. Update the planner node in `src/graph/nodes/planner.py` to recall memory before invoking the LLM:
   python
   from src.tools.memory_recall import memory_recall

   def planner_node(state: ResearchState) -> ResearchState:
       memory_context = memory_recall.invoke(
           {"query": state["query"], "k": 5}
       )
       prompt = PLANNER_PROMPT.format(
           memory_context=memory_context,
           query=state["query"],
       )
       plan = llm.invoke(prompt)
       return {**state, "plan": plan.content}
   
3. Ensure the planner does not crash when the ChromaDB collection does not yet exist (the `memory_recall` tool already handles this — verify the empty case).
4. Add a unit test `tests/test_planner_memory.py`:
   - Pre-populate ChromaDB with a finding about `"quantum computing"`
   - Invoke the planner node with query `"quantum computing basics"`
   - Assert the resulting plan string contains a phrase like `"already covered"` or `"prior research"` OR assert it does NOT include sub-questions about already-known sub-topics
5. Add a regression test with empty ChromaDB asserting the planner still returns a non-empty plan.

## Context
Without this integration, the agent would re-search the web for topics it already researched in a previous session — wasting API calls and producing duplicate content. This RAG-augmented planning pattern (retrieve → augment prompt → generate) is the core of M3. In Java terms, it is like checking a local cache before making a remote service call. The planner is the highest-leverage injection point because it controls what sub-tasks are even attempted.

## References
- [LangChain RAG How-To: Augmenting Prompts](https://python.langchain.com/docs/how_to/qa_chat_history_how_to/) — pattern for injecting retrieved context into prompts
- [LangGraph Node Implementation Guide](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/how-tos/state-reducers/) — how nodes read and write state
- [LangSmith Tracing Overview](https://docs.smith.langchain.com/tracing) — verifying tool calls appear in traces

issues.title: Build incremental research mode: "tell me more about [prev topic]"
issues.description:
## User Story
As a user, I want to ask the agent to "tell me more about [previous topic]" and have it automatically recall and build on prior research, so that I can deepen a specific area without re-running a full research session.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The CLI accepts a `--thread-id` flag to resume a prior session (already done in M3-2; this issue extends the *behavior*, not just state restore)
- [ ] When the agent detects a query of the form `"more about X"` or `"expand on X"`, it calls `memory_recall` with topic X before planning
- [ ] The resulting research plan explicitly references prior findings and generates only gap-filling sub-questions
- [ ] End-to-end test: run the agent twice on the same thread; the second run's plan references memory from the first run
- [ ] A new `incremental` mode flag `--mode incremental` is accepted by the CLI (alternative to full re-research)

## Technical Tasks
1. Add a query intent classifier in `src/graph/nodes/planner.py`. A lightweight LLM call (or regex) detects incremental intent:
   python
   import re

   INCREMENTAL_PATTERNS = [
       r"more about", r"expand on", r"tell me more", r"deeper dive into", r"follow up on"
   ]

   def is_incremental_query(query: str) -> bool:
       return any(re.search(p, query, re.IGNORECASE) for p in INCREMENTAL_PATTERNS)
   
2. In the planner node, branch on incremental vs. full research:
   python
   def planner_node(state: ResearchState) -> ResearchState:
       if is_incremental_query(state["query"]):
           # Aggressive memory recall: higher k, no topic filter
           memory_context = memory_recall.invoke({"query": state["query"], "k": 10})
           prompt = INCREMENTAL_PLANNER_PROMPT.format(
               memory_context=memory_context, query=state["query"]
           )
       else:
           memory_context = memory_recall.invoke({"query": state["query"], "k": 5})
           prompt = PLANNER_PROMPT.format(
               memory_context=memory_context, query=state["query"]
           )
       ...
   
3. Add `INCREMENTAL_PLANNER_PROMPT` to `src/prompts/planner.py`. It should instruct the LLM to treat memory as ground truth and only generate sub-questions for gaps:
   
   You are continuing prior research. The following findings are already known:
   {memory_context}
   Generate 2-3 sub-questions that go DEEPER or fill GAPS in the above. Do not repeat covered ground.
   Query: {query}
   
4. Add `--mode` option to `src/cli.py`:
   python
   @click.option("--mode", default="full", type=click.Choice(["full", "incremental"]))
   
   Pass `mode` into the initial graph state so the planner can also use it as a signal alongside the query text.
5. Write an end-to-end test `tests/test_incremental.py` using a real (temp) ChromaDB and SQLite checkpointer: first invocation stores findings, second invocation with `"tell me more about X"` produces a plan referencing those findings.

## Context
This feature turns the agent from a one-shot tool into an iterative research assistant. It combines both memory systems introduced in M3: the SQLite checkpointer (conversation continuity within a session) and ChromaDB (semantic recall across sessions). The UX mental model is similar to a human research assistant saying "Based on what we found last time, let me now dig into the gaps."

## References
- [LangGraph State Management](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/concepts/low_level/#state) — how to add new fields to graph state
- [LangChain Prompt Templates](https://python.langchain.com/docs/concepts/prompt_templates/) — structuring multi-variable prompts
- [Python re module](https://docs.python.org/3/library/re.html) — regex for intent detection

issues.title: Add memory management CLI commands: list, search, prune
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer or power user, I want CLI commands to list, search, and prune stored memory, so that I can inspect what the agent has learned and remove outdated or irrelevant findings.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `uv run python -m src.cli memory list` prints all stored memory entries (id, source, topic, timestamp, content snippet) in a formatted table
- [ ] `uv run python -m src.cli memory search "<query>"` retrieves and displays top-5 semantically similar entries
- [ ] `uv run python -m src.cli memory prune --before <ISO-date>` deletes all entries with `timestamp` older than the given date and prints how many were removed
- [ ] `uv run python -m src.cli memory prune --topic <topic>` deletes all entries for a given topic
- [ ] All commands use Rich for formatted output (tables, colored output)
- [ ] Unit test `tests/test_memory_cli.py` invokes each command via Click's `CliRunner` and asserts exit code 0 and expected output substrings

## Technical Tasks
1. Add a `memory` Click command group to `src/cli.py`:
   python
   import click
   from rich.console import Console
   from rich.table import Table

   console = Console()

   @cli.group()
   def memory():
       """Manage agent long-term memory."""
       pass
   
2. Implement `memory list`:
   python
   @memory.command("list")
   def memory_list():
       col = get_collection("research")
       results = col.get(include=["documents", "metadatas"])
       table = Table(title="Stored Memory")
       table.add_column("ID", style="dim"); table.add_column("Source")
       table.add_column("Topic"); table.add_column("Snippet")
       for id_, doc, meta in zip(results["ids"], results["documents"], results["metadatas"]):
           table.add_row(id_[:8], meta.get("source",""), meta.get("topic",""), doc[:60])
       console.print(table)
   
3. Implement `memory search`:
   python
   @memory.command("search")
   @click.argument("query")
   def memory_search(query: str):
       result = memory_recall.invoke({"query": query, "k": 5})
       console.print(result)
   
4. Implement `memory prune`:
   python
   @memory.command("prune")
   @click.option("--before", default=None, help="ISO date, e.g. 2025-01-01")
   @click.option("--topic", default=None)
   def memory_prune(before: str | None, topic: str | None):
       col = get_collection("research")
       # Build ChromaDB `where` filter
       where: dict = {}
       if before:
           import datetime
           cutoff = int(datetime.datetime.fromisoformat(before).timestamp())
           where["timestamp"] = {"$lt": cutoff}
       if topic:
           where["topic"] = topic
       results = col.get(where=where if where else None)
       ids_to_delete = results["ids"]
       if ids_to_delete:
           col._collection.delete(ids=ids_to_delete)
       console.print(f"[green]Pruned {len(ids_to_delete)} entries.[/green]")
   
5. Write `tests/test_memory_cli.py` using Click's `CliRunner` and a `tmp_path` ChromaDB fixture.

## Context
Without management tooling, the vector store grows unboundedly and old/wrong findings pollute future recall. These commands follow the principle of "if it runs in production, you need to be able to inspect and operate it." Rich is already used in the CLI for research output (M1); this issue extends that pattern to memory management. Think of it as a `jmap`/`jconsole` equivalent for the agent's memory heap.

## References
- [Click Command Groups](https://click.palletsprojects.com/en/8.x/commands/) — how to nest sub-commands
- [Rich Tables Documentation](https://rich.readthedocs.io/en/stable/tables.html) — Table, add_column, add_row
- [ChromaDB get() and delete() API](https://docs.trychroma.com/docs/querying-collections/query-and-get) — fetching and deleting by metadata filters
- [Click Testing with CliRunner](https://click.palletsprojects.com/en/8.x/testing/) — how to invoke CLI commands in unit tests

issues.title: Add ChromaDB to docker-compose.yml (server mode)
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer running the full stack locally, I want ChromaDB running as a Docker service in client-server mode, so that the vector store is isolated, persistent, and accessible by both the API server and CLI without file-locking conflicts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `docker-compose.yml` includes a `chroma` service using the `chromadb/chroma` image
- [ ] ChromaDB data is persisted to a named Docker volume (`chroma-data`)
- [ ] The application connects to ChromaDB in HTTP client mode when `CHROMA_MODE=server` env var is set; falls back to embedded (local file) mode when unset
- [ ] `docker compose up` starts the agent API and ChromaDB; `curl http://localhost:8000/api/v1/heartbeat` returns 200
- [ ] `src/memory/vector_store.py` is updated to switch between embedded and HTTP client based on env var
- [ ] `.env.example` documents `CHROMA_MODE`, `CHROMA_HOST`, and `CHROMA_PORT`

## Technical Tasks
1. Add the `chroma` service to `docker-compose.yml`:
   yaml
   services:
     chroma:
       image: chromadb/chroma:latest
       ports:
         - "8000:8000"
       volumes:
         - chroma-data:/chroma/chroma
       environment:
         - IS_PERSISTENT=TRUE

     api:
       build: .
       ports:
         - "8080:8080"
       depends_on:
         - chroma
       environment:
         - CHROMA_MODE=server
         - CHROMA_HOST=chroma
         - CHROMA_PORT=8000

   volumes:
     chroma-data:
   
2. Update `src/memory/vector_store.py` to support both modes:
   python
   import os
   import chromadb

   def _get_chroma_client() -> chromadb.ClientAPI:
       if os.getenv("CHROMA_MODE") == "server":
           return chromadb.HttpClient(
               host=os.getenv("CHROMA_HOST", "localhost"),
               port=int(os.getenv("CHROMA_PORT", "8000")),
           )
       # Default: embedded (file-based), used in local dev and tests
       CHROMA_PATH.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
       return chromadb.PersistentClient(path=str(CHROMA_PATH))
   
   This is analogous to a JDBC URL switch between `localhost` and a Docker service hostname.
3. Add to `.env.example`:
   
   # ChromaDB connection (leave unset for local embedded mode)
   CHROMA_MODE=server
   CHROMA_HOST=localhost
   CHROMA_PORT=8000
   
4. Verify the setup: `docker compose up -d chroma && curl http://localhost:8000/api/v1/heartbeat`
5. Update `docker-compose.yml` health check so the API service waits for ChromaDB to be ready:
   yaml
   chroma:
     healthcheck:
       test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:8000/api/v1/heartbeat"]
       interval: 5s
       retries: 5
   

## Context
Running ChromaDB in embedded mode works fine for a single process but causes file-locking errors when both the CLI and the API server try to open the same SQLite-backed files simultaneously. Client-server mode routes all access through ChromaDB's HTTP API, eliminating the conflict. Named Docker volumes ensure data survives container restarts. This pattern mirrors how production Java apps connect to a separately deployed database rather than an embedded H2 instance.

## References
- [ChromaDB Docker Deployment Guide](https://docs.trychroma.com/production/containers/docker) — official Docker image, environment variables, volume configuration
- [ChromaDB HttpClient API](https://docs.trychroma.com/reference/py-client) — `chromadb.HttpClient` constructor parameters
- [Docker Compose depends_on with healthcheck](https://docs.docker.com/compose/how-tos/startup-order/) — ensuring service startup order

issues.title: Make embedding model configurable (OpenAI / local sentence-transformers)
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want the embedding model to be configurable via `configs/models.yaml` so that I can switch between OpenAI's cloud embeddings and local sentence-transformers without changing application code.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `configs/models.yaml` has an `embeddings` section with `provider`, `model`, and optional `api_key_env` fields
- [ ] `src/memory/vector_store.py` reads the config and instantiates the correct embedding function at startup
- [ ] Setting `provider: openai` and running the agent uses `text-embedding-3-small` via the OpenAI API
- [ ] Setting `provider: sentence-transformers` uses the local model with no API calls (verified by running offline)
- [ ] Switching providers requires only a config file change — no code changes
- [ ] Unit test `tests/test_embedding_config.py` asserts that both providers return a list of floats for a test string

## Technical Tasks
1. Update `configs/models.yaml`:
   yaml
   embeddings:
     provider: sentence-transformers   # Options: openai, sentence-transformers
     model: all-MiniLM-L6-v2
     # For OpenAI:
     # provider: openai
     # model: text-embedding-3-small
     # api_key_env: OPENAI_API_KEY
   
2. Add OpenAI embeddings dependency (it may already be present):
   bash
   uv add langchain-openai
   
3. Update `src/memory/vector_store.py` to read config and build the correct embedding function:
   python
   import yaml
   from pathlib import Path

   def get_embedding_function():
       config = yaml.safe_load(Path("configs/models.yaml").read_text())
       emb_cfg = config["embeddings"]
       provider = emb_cfg["provider"]
       model = emb_cfg["model"]

       if provider == "openai":
           from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
           return OpenAIEmbeddings(model=model)
       elif provider == "sentence-transformers":
           from langchain_community.embeddings import SentenceTransformerEmbeddings
           return SentenceTransformerEmbeddings(model_name=model)
       else:
           raise ValueError(f"Unknown embedding provider: {provider}")
   
   This is the Strategy pattern: `get_embedding_function()` selects the implementation at runtime based on config, analogous to a Spring `@ConditionalOnProperty` bean.
4. Cache the embedding function (avoid reloading model on every call):
   python
   from functools import lru_cache

   @lru_cache(maxsize=1)
   def get_embedding_function(): ...
   
5. Write `tests/test_embedding_config.py`. Mock the YAML config using `monkeypatch` or a temp config file to test both branches without requiring an OpenAI API key in CI.

## Context
Hardcoding the embedding model creates vendor lock-in and makes local development expensive for contributors without an OpenAI key. Configuration-driven provider selection follows the same pattern used for LLM providers in `configs/models.yaml`. sentence-transformers is the correct default for development (free, fast enough, no internet required); OpenAI embeddings produce higher-quality vectors and should be used for production deployments where retrieval quality matters more than cost.

## References
- [OpenAI text-embedding-3-small Model Card](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/embeddings/embedding-models) — dimensions, pricing, use cases
- [langchain-openai OpenAIEmbeddings](https://python.langchain.com/docs/integrations/text_embedding/openai/) — constructor parameters, model selection
- [sentence-transformers Model Hub](https://www.sbert.net/docs/sentence_transformer/pretrained_models.html) — all-MiniLM-L6-v2 specs and alternatives
- [Python functools.lru_cache](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functools.html#functools.lru_cache) — caching expensive initialization calls

issues.title: ADR-003: Memory architecture and retention policy
issues.description:
## User Story
As a team member, I want an ADR documenting the M3 memory architecture and retention policy, so that future contributors understand why these design choices were made and how to evolve them safely.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `docs/adr/ADR-003-memory-architecture.md` exists and follows the MADR template in `docs/adr/template.md`
- [ ] The ADR covers: choice of ChromaDB over alternatives (Pinecone, Qdrant, pgvector), choice of SQLite checkpointer over Postgres for M3, embedding model selection rationale
- [ ] The ADR defines a concrete retention policy: maximum collection size, age-based pruning schedule, and the prune command to use
- [ ] The ADR documents the two-memory-system design (checkpointer for conversation state + vector store for semantic memory) and explains why they are separate
- [ ] Status is set to `Accepted`
- [ ] ADR is linked from `README.md` under an Architecture Decisions section

## Technical Tasks
1. Copy `docs/adr/template.md` to `docs/adr/ADR-003-memory-architecture.md`.
2. Fill in all MADR sections. Suggested content for key sections:

   **Context and Problem Statement:**
   The agent needs two forms of memory: (1) intra-session conversation history (which nodes were visited, what was planned) and (2) inter-session semantic recall (what topics were researched and what was found). These have different access patterns and different storage requirements.

   **Decision Drivers:**
   - Zero-infrastructure requirement for M3 (no managed cloud services)
   - Must be swappable at M5 without application code changes
   - Local development must work offline (rules out Pinecone/OpenAI embeddings as hard dependencies)

   **Considered Options:**
   - ChromaDB (embedded) vs. Qdrant (Docker-only) vs. pgvector (requires Postgres)
   - SQLite checkpointer vs. in-memory checkpointer vs. Postgres checkpointer

   **Decision Outcome:**
   ChromaDB embedded for M3 (promoted to server mode in M3-9 for multi-process), SQLite for checkpointing. Both are swappable via env var / config.

   **Retention Policy:**
   - Max 10,000 chunks per collection
   - Prune entries older than 90 days: `uv run python -m src.cli memory prune --before $(date -d '90 days ago' +%Y-%m-%d)`
   - Prune by topic when a research area is explicitly retired

3. Add to `README.md`:
   markdown
   ## Architecture Decisions
   - [ADR-003: Memory Architecture and Retention Policy](docs/adr/ADR-003-memory-architecture.md)
   
4. Commit with message: `docs: add ADR-003 memory architecture and retention policy`

## Context
ADRs (Architecture Decision Records) are lightweight documents that capture *why* a decision was made, not just *what* was decided. They are especially valuable when a new team member asks "why are we using ChromaDB and not Pinecone?" — the ADR provides the answer without requiring a meeting. The MADR (Markdown Architectural Decision Records) format is already established in this project via the template. This is the third ADR; ADR-001 and ADR-002 covered earlier milestones.

## References
- [MADR — Markdown Architectural Decision Records](https://adr.github.io/madr/) — format specification and examples
- [LangGraph Checkpointer Backends](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/concepts/persistence/#checkpointer-libraries) — list of available backends and their trade-offs
- [ChromaDB vs. alternatives comparison](https://docs.trychroma.com/guides) — positioning relative to other vector stores

issues.title: Spike: LangGraph multi-agent patterns (supervisor, swarm, network)
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer new to LangGraph, I want to understand the available multi-agent coordination patterns (supervisor, swarm, network) so that I can make an informed architectural decision for the M4 multi-agent system.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A written spike report (in `docs/til/` or `docs/planning/`) summarizes supervisor, swarm, and network patterns with pros/cons for each
- [ ] The report includes a recommendation for which pattern to use in this project, with justification
- [ ] A minimal runnable code prototype (scratch script or notebook) demonstrates the chosen pattern with at least two agents exchanging a message
- [ ] The spike report is committed and linked from the M4 ADR planning notes

## Technical Tasks
1. Read the LangGraph multi-agent conceptual docs: https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/concepts/multi_agent/
2. Study the three coordination patterns:
   - **Supervisor:** A central LLM routes tasks to worker agents and collects results. Best for orchestrated delegation.
   - **Swarm:** Agents hand off to each other peer-to-peer based on their own judgment. Best for emergent workflows.
   - **Network:** Arbitrary agent-to-agent messaging. Most flexible, most complex.
3. Read the prebuilt supervisor helper docs: https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/how-tos/langgraph-platform/
4. Write a comparison table in `docs/planning/m4_multiagent_spike.md` covering: routing mechanism, statefulness, complexity, fit for research pipeline.
5. Build a minimal prototype in `scripts/spike_multiagent.py`:
   python
   # Example: supervisor routing to a dummy researcher agent
   from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
   # Define minimal state, two nodes, supervisor routing logic
   
6. Run the prototype: `uv run python scripts/spike_multiagent.py`
7. Record your recommendation and rationale in the spike doc.

## Context
M4 introduces multi-agent collaboration to replace the single monolithic ReAct agent from M1–M3. The architectural pattern chosen here locks in how agents communicate for the rest of the project. In Java terms, think of this as choosing between a message broker (supervisor), a chain-of-responsibility pattern (swarm), or a full event bus (network). The supervisor pattern is the most common starting point for structured research pipelines.

## References
- [LangGraph Multi-Agent Concepts](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/concepts/multi_agent/) — explains supervisor, swarm, and network patterns with diagrams
- [LangGraph Multi-Agent Tutorials](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/tutorials/multi_agent/multi-agent-collaboration/) — runnable examples of each pattern
- [LangGraph Prebuilt Supervisor](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/how-tos/langgraph-platform/) — higher-level helper for building supervisor graphs

issues.title: Define agent roles and contracts: Planner, Researcher, Analyst, Writer
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want to define clear roles and communication contracts for the Planner, Researcher, Analyst, and Writer agents so that each agent has a focused responsibility and agents can be developed and tested independently.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A role definition document exists at `docs/planning/agent_roles.md` describing each agent's purpose, inputs, outputs, and constraints
- [ ] Each agent has a dedicated system prompt file under `src/prompts/` (e.g. `researcher.py`, `analyst.py`, `writer.py`, `planner.py`)
- [ ] Each system prompt is a Python string constant (not hardcoded inline in agent code)
- [ ] A Pydantic model in `src/models/` defines the handoff message schema between agents (see M4-7 for full implementation)
- [ ] At least one unit test per agent verifies that the system prompt is non-empty and contains expected keywords
- [ ] All files pass `uv run ruff check .` and `uv run mypy src/`

## Technical Tasks
1. Create `docs/planning/agent_roles.md` with this structure for each agent:
   
   ### Researcher
   - **Role:** Gather raw information from the web on a specific sub-topic
   - **Input:** A focused research question string
   - **Output:** List of findings with source URLs
   - **Must NOT:** Synthesize, editorialize, or write prose
   
2. Create system prompt constants in `src/prompts/`:
   python
   # src/prompts/researcher.py
   RESEARCHER_SYSTEM_PROMPT = """
   You are a focused web researcher. Given a specific research question,
   your ONLY job is to search the web and collect relevant facts and sources.
   Do NOT synthesize or write conclusions. Return structured findings.
   """
   
3. Define the four agent roles: Planner (decomposes query into sub-tasks), Researcher (searches web), Analyst (synthesizes findings), Writer (produces final report).
4. Create a stub Pydantic model for agent messages in `src/models/agent_messages.py`:
   python
   from pydantic import BaseModel
   class ResearchTask(BaseModel):
       task_id: str
       question: str
       assigned_to: str
   
5. Write tests in `tests/test_prompts.py`:
   python
   from src.prompts.researcher import RESEARCHER_SYSTEM_PROMPT
   def test_researcher_prompt_not_empty():
       assert len(RESEARCHER_SYSTEM_PROMPT.strip()) > 50
   
6. Run: `uv run pytest tests/test_prompts.py -v`

## Context
In software engineering terms, this issue defines the API contracts between agents before any implementation. This is analogous to writing Java interfaces before writing implementing classes — it prevents tight coupling and enables parallel development. Each agent should be testable in isolation by injecting a mock LLM.

## References
- [LangChain System Messages](https://python.langchain.com/docs/concepts/messages/#systemmessage) — how system prompts are passed to LangChain chat models
- [Pydantic Models](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/models/) — defining typed data schemas in Python (analogous to Java POJOs with validation)

issues.title: Implement Supervisor node: orchestrate agent delegation
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want to implement a Supervisor node in LangGraph that receives the user query, delegates sub-tasks to the appropriate specialized agents, and aggregates their results so that the system can coordinate complex research workflows.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A `SupervisorNode` class or function exists in `src/graph/supervisor.py`
- [ ] The supervisor routes tasks to at least Researcher and Analyst agents based on LLM decision
- [ ] The supervisor terminates the graph and returns to `END` when all tasks are complete
- [ ] A LangGraph `StateGraph` in `src/graph/multi_agent_graph.py` wires the supervisor and worker agent nodes
- [ ] An integration test in `tests/test_supervisor.py` sends a simple query and asserts the graph reaches `END` without error (can use a mocked LLM)
- [ ] All code passes `uv run ruff check .` and `uv run mypy src/`

## Technical Tasks
1. Create `src/graph/supervisor.py`. The supervisor node reads the current state and calls the LLM to decide which agent should act next:
   python
   from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
   from typing import Literal

   def supervisor_node(state: AgentState) -> dict:
       # Call LLM with supervisor system prompt
       # LLM returns: {"next": "researcher" | "analyst" | "writer" | "FINISH"}
       response = llm.invoke(supervisor_prompt + state["messages"])
       next_agent = parse_routing_decision(response)
       return {"next": next_agent}
   
2. Define a routing function that reads `state["next"]` and returns the next node name:
   python
   def route(state: AgentState) -> Literal["researcher", "analyst", "writer", END]:
       return END if state["next"] == "FINISH" else state["next"]
   
3. Wire the graph in `src/graph/multi_agent_graph.py`:
   python
   builder = StateGraph(AgentState)
   builder.add_node("supervisor", supervisor_node)
   builder.add_node("researcher", researcher_node)  # stub for now
   builder.set_entry_point("supervisor")
   builder.add_conditional_edges("supervisor", route)
   builder.add_edge("researcher", "supervisor")  # always return to supervisor
   graph = builder.compile()
   
4. The `AgentState` TypedDict must include a `next` field:
   python
   from typing import TypedDict, Annotated
   from langgraph.graph.message import add_messages
   class AgentState(TypedDict):
       messages: Annotated[list, add_messages]
       next: str
   
5. Write a test with a mocked LLM that returns a fixed routing decision, verify graph traversal.
6. Run the graph manually: `uv run python -c "from src.graph.multi_agent_graph import graph; print(graph.get_graph().draw_ascii())"`

## Context
The supervisor is the central nervous system of the multi-agent pipeline. In Java terms, it is equivalent to an orchestrator in a microservices saga pattern — it holds the global workflow state and decides what to do next. Unlike a simple chain, the supervisor can loop: it sends a task, receives results, and decides if more work is needed before finishing.

## References
- [LangGraph Supervisor How-To](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/how-tos/multi-agent-supervisor/) — step-by-step supervisor implementation guide
- [LangGraph Conditional Edges](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/concepts/low_level/#conditional-edges) — how routing functions work
- [LangGraph State Management](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/concepts/low_level/#state) — TypedDict-based state and reducers

issues.title: Extract Researcher agent: web search + data gathering specialist
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want to extract a dedicated Researcher agent that specializes in web search and data gathering so that this capability is encapsulated, independently testable, and can be invoked by the supervisor.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A `researcher_node` function exists in `src/agents/researcher.py`
- [ ] The Researcher uses the existing `web_search` and `web_fetch` tools from M1
- [ ] The Researcher reads its assigned task from agent state and writes structured findings back to state
- [ ] The Researcher does NOT synthesize or draw conclusions — it only collects and returns raw data with sources
- [ ] A unit test in `tests/agents/test_researcher.py` mocks the search tools and asserts the output structure is correct
- [ ] All code passes `uv run ruff check .` and `uv run mypy src/`

## Technical Tasks
1. Create `src/agents/researcher.py`:
   python
   from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
   from src.prompts.researcher import RESEARCHER_SYSTEM_PROMPT
   from src.tools.search import web_search  # existing tool from M1
   from src.models.agent_messages import ResearchTask, ResearchFindings

   def researcher_node(state: AgentState) -> dict:
       task: ResearchTask = state["current_task"]
       # Bind tools to LLM and invoke
       agent = llm.bind_tools([web_search, web_fetch])
       result = agent.invoke([
           SystemMessage(RESEARCHER_SYSTEM_PROMPT),
           HumanMessage(task.question)
       ])
       # Parse tool calls and execute them
       findings = execute_tool_calls(result)
       return {"research_findings": findings, "next": "supervisor"}
   
2. Extend `src/models/agent_messages.py` with a `ResearchFindings` model:
   python
   class ResearchFindings(BaseModel):
       task_id: str
       findings: list[str]
       sources: list[str]  # URLs
   
3. Create `src/agents/__init__.py` if it does not exist.
4. Write a unit test mocking `web_search` to return a fixed result, assert `ResearchFindings` is populated.
5. Register the researcher node in the supervisor graph from M4-3.
6. Run: `uv run pytest tests/agents/test_researcher.py -v`

## Context
Extracting the Researcher into its own module applies the Single Responsibility Principle — a concept familiar from Java. Previously, the M1 ReAct agent handled searching, analysis, and writing all in one loop. Splitting these roles makes each part easier to test, tune, and replace. The Researcher is intentionally restricted to data collection; synthesis is the Analyst's job.

## References
- [LangChain Tool Calling](https://python.langchain.com/docs/concepts/tool_calling/) — how to bind tools to a chat model and handle tool call results
- [LangChain Agents with Tool Use](https://python.langchain.com/docs/how_to/agent_executor/) — patterns for agents that call tools

issues.title: Extract Analyst agent: synthesize, compare, identify patterns
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want to extract a dedicated Analyst agent that synthesizes research findings, compares information across sources, and identifies patterns so that the Researcher's raw data is transformed into structured insight before the Writer produces the final report.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] An `analyst_node` function exists in `src/agents/analyst.py`
- [ ] The Analyst reads `ResearchFindings` from agent state (produced by the Researcher)
- [ ] The Analyst produces a structured `AnalysisResult` Pydantic model written back to state
- [ ] The `AnalysisResult` includes: key themes, contradictions found, confidence level, and a brief synthesis paragraph
- [ ] A unit test in `tests/agents/test_analyst.py` mocks the LLM and asserts the `AnalysisResult` output structure
- [ ] All code passes `uv run ruff check .` and `uv run mypy src/`

## Technical Tasks
1. Create `src/agents/analyst.py`:
   python
   from src.prompts.analyst import ANALYST_SYSTEM_PROMPT
   from src.models.agent_messages import ResearchFindings, AnalysisResult

   def analyst_node(state: AgentState) -> dict:
       findings: list[ResearchFindings] = state["research_findings"]
       # Format findings into a prompt
       findings_text = format_findings(findings)
       result = llm.invoke([
           SystemMessage(ANALYST_SYSTEM_PROMPT),
           HumanMessage(f"Analyze these findings:\n{findings_text}")
       ])
       analysis = AnalysisResult.model_validate_json(result.content)
       return {"analysis_result": analysis, "next": "supervisor"}
   
2. Add `AnalysisResult` to `src/models/agent_messages.py`:
   python
   class AnalysisResult(BaseModel):
       key_themes: list[str]
       contradictions: list[str]
       confidence: float  # 0.0 to 1.0
       synthesis: str
   
3. Create `src/prompts/analyst.py` with a system prompt that instructs the LLM to only synthesize — not search or write final prose.
4. Use LangChain's structured output feature to guarantee valid JSON from the LLM:
   python
   structured_llm = llm.with_structured_output(AnalysisResult)
   
5. Write a unit test: mock `structured_llm.invoke` to return a fixed `AnalysisResult`, assert all fields are populated.
6. Register the analyst node in the supervisor graph.

## Context
The Analyst is the reasoning layer of the pipeline. In traditional software terms, this is analogous to a data transformation service — it takes raw inputs (search results), applies business logic (synthesis, comparison), and produces a typed output (AnalysisResult). Using `with_structured_output` is like enforcing a DTO (Data Transfer Object) contract: the LLM must return data conforming to the Pydantic schema or an error is raised.

## References
- [LangChain Structured Output](https://python.langchain.com/docs/how_to/structured_output/) — how to force LLM output to match a Pydantic model
- [Pydantic model_validate_json](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/serialization/#modelmodel_validate_json) — parsing JSON strings into Pydantic models

issues.title: Extract Writer agent: produce final structured report
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want to extract a dedicated Writer agent that takes the Analyst's structured analysis and produces a final, well-formatted research report with citations so that the pipeline delivers a consistent, human-readable output.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A `writer_node` function exists in `src/agents/writer.py`
- [ ] The Writer reads `AnalysisResult` and original `ResearchFindings` (for citations) from agent state
- [ ] The Writer produces a `FinalReport` Pydantic model written back to state
- [ ] `FinalReport` includes: title, executive summary, main sections (list), citations (list of source URLs), and generated timestamp
- [ ] A unit test in `tests/agents/test_writer.py` mocks the LLM and asserts the `FinalReport` structure
- [ ] The CLI (M1) still works end-to-end after this change: `uv run python -m src.cli` produces a report
- [ ] All code passes `uv run ruff check .` and `uv run mypy src/`

## Technical Tasks
1. Create `src/agents/writer.py`:
   python
   from src.prompts.writer import WRITER_SYSTEM_PROMPT
   from src.models.agent_messages import AnalysisResult, ResearchFindings, FinalReport
   import datetime

   def writer_node(state: AgentState) -> dict:
       analysis: AnalysisResult = state["analysis_result"]
       findings: list[ResearchFindings] = state["research_findings"]
       all_sources = [url for f in findings for url in f.sources]

       structured_llm = llm.with_structured_output(FinalReport)
       report = structured_llm.invoke([
           SystemMessage(WRITER_SYSTEM_PROMPT),
           HumanMessage(f"Write a report based on:\n{analysis.synthesis}")
       ])
       report.citations = all_sources  # inject real sources
       report.generated_at = datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat()
       return {"final_report": report, "next": "FINISH"}
   
2. Add `FinalReport` to `src/models/agent_messages.py`:
   python
   class FinalReport(BaseModel):
       title: str
       executive_summary: str
       sections: list[str]
       citations: list[str]
       generated_at: str
   
3. Create `src/prompts/writer.py` with a prompt emphasizing clear structure, factual accuracy, and attribution.
4. Update the supervisor routing to go to `END` after the Writer finishes.
5. Update `src/cli.py` to print `state["final_report"]` in a readable format.
6. Run end-to-end: `uv run python -m src.cli` with a test query and verify a `FinalReport` is printed.

## Context
The Writer is the final stage of the pipeline. This separation means the output format can evolve independently of the research or analysis logic — for example, switching from Markdown to HTML or PDF requires only changing the Writer. This is the Open/Closed Principle: the pipeline is open for extension (new output formats) but closed for modification (search and analysis code is untouched).

## References
- [LangChain Structured Output](https://python.langchain.com/docs/how_to/structured_output/) — ensuring the Writer returns a valid typed report object
- [Python datetime module](https://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) — generating ISO 8601 timestamps

issues.title: Implement structured handoff message protocol between agents
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want a typed, validated message protocol for all inter-agent communication so that agents cannot pass malformed data to each other, making bugs easier to catch at the boundary rather than deep inside an agent.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All inter-agent data models are defined in `src/models/agent_messages.py` using Pydantic v2
- [ ] Models cover the full pipeline: `ResearchTask`, `ResearchFindings`, `AnalysisResult`, `FinalReport`
- [ ] Every model field has a type annotation and a `Field(description=...)` docstring
- [ ] Agent nodes read and write only these typed models to/from LangGraph state — no raw dicts for inter-agent data
- [ ] A test in `tests/test_agent_messages.py` validates that invalid data raises a `ValidationError`
- [ ] All code passes `uv run ruff check .` and `uv run mypy src/`

## Technical Tasks
1. Consolidate all message models in `src/models/agent_messages.py` with full field documentation:
   python
   from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
   from typing import Optional

   class ResearchTask(BaseModel):
       task_id: str = Field(description="Unique identifier for this research task")
       question: str = Field(description="The specific research question to answer")
       assigned_to: str = Field(description="Agent role to handle this task")
       priority: int = Field(default=1, ge=1, le=5, description="Task priority 1 (high) to 5 (low)")

   class ResearchFindings(BaseModel):
       task_id: str = Field(description="Matches the originating ResearchTask")
       findings: list[str] = Field(description="List of factual findings")
       sources: list[str] = Field(description="Source URLs for each finding")
   
2. Add `model_config = ConfigDict(strict=True)` to each model to prevent coercion of wrong types.
3. Update all agent nodes from M4-4, M4-5, M4-6 to use these models (not raw dicts).
4. Update `AgentState` TypedDict to reference these models:
   python
   class AgentState(TypedDict):
       messages: Annotated[list, add_messages]
       current_task: Optional[ResearchTask]
       research_findings: list[ResearchFindings]
       analysis_result: Optional[AnalysisResult]
       final_report: Optional[FinalReport]
       next: str
   
5. Write validation tests:
   python
   import pytest
   from pydantic import ValidationError
   from src.models.agent_messages import ResearchTask

   def test_research_task_rejects_missing_question():
       with pytest.raises(ValidationError):
           ResearchTask(task_id="1", assigned_to="researcher")  # missing question
   
6. Run: `uv run pytest tests/test_agent_messages.py -v`

## Context
In Java, you would define interfaces or DTOs with JSR-303 bean validation annotations (`@NotNull`, `@Size`) to enforce data contracts between services. Pydantic v2 is the Python equivalent — it validates data at object construction time and raises a clear `ValidationError` immediately if a contract is violated. This fail-fast behavior prevents cryptic errors from propagating deep into the pipeline.

## References
- [Pydantic v2 Models](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/models/) — defining and validating models
- [Pydantic Field Validation](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/fields/) — Field descriptors, constraints, and descriptions
- [Pydantic ConfigDict](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/config/) — strict mode and other model configuration

issues.title: Support parallel research: multiple Researchers on different sub-tasks
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want the system to dispatch multiple Researcher agents in parallel on different sub-tasks so that complex research queries that require gathering information on several independent topics complete faster.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The supervisor can fan out to N Researcher agents in parallel, where N is determined at runtime by the number of sub-tasks
- [ ] Each parallel Researcher receives its own `ResearchTask` and writes its own `ResearchFindings` to a shared list in state
- [ ] The graph only proceeds to the Analyst after ALL parallel researchers have completed
- [ ] A test in `tests/test_parallel_research.py` verifies that two tasks dispatched in parallel both appear in `state["research_findings"]`
- [ ] All code passes `uv run ruff check .` and `uv run mypy src/`

## Technical Tasks
1. Read the LangGraph Send API docs: https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/how-tos/map-reduce/
2. The Send API enables dynamic fan-out — you send a different `ResearchTask` to the same node N times in parallel:
   python
   from langgraph.types import Send

   def dispatch_research(state: AgentState) -> list[Send]:
       """Fan out: one Send per sub-task."""
       return [
           Send("researcher", {**state, "current_task": task})
           for task in state["pending_tasks"]
       ]
   
3. Add the fan-out edge from the supervisor to the researcher using `add_conditional_edges`:
   python
   builder.add_conditional_edges(
       "supervisor",
       dispatch_research,
       ["researcher"]  # possible target node(s)
   )
   
4. The `research_findings` field in `AgentState` must use a reducer that appends (not overwrites) results from parallel branches:
   python
   import operator
   class AgentState(TypedDict):
       research_findings: Annotated[list[ResearchFindings], operator.add]
   
5. After all researchers complete, add an edge that routes to the analyst:
   python
   builder.add_edge("researcher", "analyst")
   
   LangGraph automatically waits for all parallel branches to complete before advancing.
6. Test with two mock tasks: assert both `ResearchFindings` are present in final state.
7. Run: `uv run pytest tests/test_parallel_research.py -v`

## Context
This is LangGraph's map-reduce pattern: the supervisor maps a list of tasks to parallel worker executions, then LangGraph automatically reduces the results by merging them using the state reducer. In Java, this is conceptually similar to using `CompletableFuture.allOf()` to run multiple async tasks and join their results. The `operator.add` reducer is key — without it, parallel branches would overwrite each other's state.

## References
- [LangGraph Send API / Map-Reduce](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/how-tos/map-reduce/) — the primary guide for parallel fan-out with dynamic task dispatch
- [LangGraph State Reducers](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/concepts/low_level/#reducers) — how to define merge behavior for parallel branch results
- [Python operator module](https://docs.python.org/3/library/operator.html) — `operator.add` used as a list append reducer

issues.title: Add per-agent observability: agent identity in LangSmith traces
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want each agent's execution to be tagged with its identity in LangSmith traces so that I can distinguish Researcher, Analyst, and Writer runs in the observability dashboard and debug multi-agent workflows efficiently.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Each agent node invokes the LLM with a `run_name` identifying the agent (e.g. `"researcher"`, `"analyst"`, `"writer"`)
- [ ] Each LLM call includes `metadata` tags with at least `agent_role` and `session_id` fields
- [ ] LangSmith traces (visible in the LangSmith UI) show distinct named runs for each agent
- [ ] A test confirms that `run_name` and `metadata` are passed correctly to the LLM invoke call (can mock the LLM)
- [ ] All code passes `uv run ruff check .` and `uv run mypy src/`

## Technical Tasks
1. Read LangSmith tracing configuration: https://docs.smith.langchain.com/how_to_guides/tracing
2. Update each agent's LLM invocation to pass `run_name` and `metadata`:
   python
   # In researcher_node:
   result = llm.invoke(
       messages,
       config={
           "run_name": "researcher",
           "metadata": {
               "agent_role": "researcher",
               "session_id": state.get("session_id", "unknown"),
               "task_id": state["current_task"].task_id,
           }
       }
   )
   
3. Pass `session_id` through `AgentState` so it is available in every agent node:
   python
   class AgentState(TypedDict):
       session_id: str  # generated at graph entry, passed to all nodes
       # ... other fields
   
4. Ensure `LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2=true` and `LANGCHAIN_API_KEY` are set in `.env` (they should be from earlier milestones).
5. Add structlog logging at the entry of each agent node:
   python
   import structlog
   log = structlog.get_logger()

   def researcher_node(state: AgentState) -> dict:
       log.info("researcher.start", task_id=state["current_task"].task_id)
       ...
   
6. Write a test asserting that `llm.invoke` is called with the expected `config` dict (use `unittest.mock.patch`).
7. Manually run a query and verify in the LangSmith UI that runs are labeled correctly.

## Context
In a distributed Java microservices system, you would use tools like Zipkin or Jaeger with trace IDs to correlate logs across services. LangSmith plays the same role for LLM agents: every LLM call is recorded, and `run_name` + `metadata` let you filter, search, and group traces by agent role. This is essential for debugging multi-agent systems where failures can occur at any stage in the pipeline.

## References
- [LangSmith Tracing How-To](https://docs.smith.langchain.com/how_to_guides/tracing) — configuring tracing and adding metadata to runs
- [LangSmith Run Metadata](https://docs.smith.langchain.com/how_to_guides/tracing/annotate_code) — adding tags, metadata, and run names to traces
- [structlog Documentation](https://www.structlog.org/en/stable/) — structured logging for Python (already in the project stack)

issues.title: End-to-end demo: complex research query through full multi-agent pipeline
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want to run a complex research query through the complete multi-agent pipeline end-to-end so that I can validate that the Supervisor, Researcher, Analyst, and Writer agents work together correctly and produce a coherent final report.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Running `uv run python -m src.cli` with a multi-faceted query (e.g. "Compare the AI research output of OpenAI and DeepMind in 2024") completes without errors
- [ ] The final output is a `FinalReport` with a non-empty title, executive summary, at least 2 sections, and at least 3 citation URLs
- [ ] LangSmith shows a trace with distinct labeled runs for supervisor, researcher (potentially multiple), analyst, and writer
- [ ] The pipeline handles at least one edge case gracefully: empty search results, LLM timeout, or malformed tool output
- [ ] A `tests/test_e2e.py` integration test (marked `@pytest.mark.integration`) runs the full graph with real or mocked services and asserts a valid `FinalReport` is returned

## Technical Tasks
1. Create a representative test query that exercises all agents:
   
   "Compare the AI research output of OpenAI and DeepMind in 2024, covering key papers, model releases, and benchmark performance."
   
2. Run the CLI manually and inspect each stage's output with `structlog` logs.
3. Add error handling in each agent node for common failure modes:
   python
   try:
       findings = execute_tool_calls(result)
   except Exception as e:
       log.error("researcher.tool_failure", error=str(e))
       findings = ResearchFindings(task_id=task.task_id, findings=[], sources=[])
   
4. Create `tests/test_e2e.py` with an integration test:
   python
   import pytest
   @pytest.mark.integration
   async def test_full_pipeline_returns_report():
       from src.graph.multi_agent_graph import graph
       state = await graph.ainvoke({"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Test query"}]})
       assert state["final_report"] is not None
       assert len(state["final_report"].sections) >= 2
   
5. Run integration tests separately: `uv run pytest tests/test_e2e.py -m integration -v`
6. Review the LangSmith trace and screenshot it for the project documentation.
7. Fix any issues found and update the spike notes in `docs/planning/`.

## Context
This is the milestone's acceptance gate. No new technology is introduced — this issue validates that all the pieces assembled in M4-1 through M4-9 work together as a system. In Java terms, this is analogous to an end-to-end integration test that spins up the full Spring Boot application and sends HTTP requests through the controller, service, and repository layers.

issues.title: ADR-004: Multi-agent coordination pattern and trade-offs
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer and future architect, I want a formal Architecture Decision Record documenting the choice of multi-agent coordination pattern and its trade-offs so that future developers understand why the supervisor pattern was chosen over alternatives.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `docs/adr/004-multi-agent-coordination.md` exists and follows the MADR format defined in `docs/adr/template.md`
- [ ] The ADR covers all three patterns considered: supervisor, swarm, and network
- [ ] The ADR includes a comparison table with columns: Pattern, Routing mechanism, Statefulness, Complexity, Fit for research pipeline
- [ ] The Consequences section lists at least 2 positive and 2 negative consequences of the chosen pattern
- [ ] The ADR status is set to "Accepted"
- [ ] The ADR is linked from `README.md` under an "Architecture Decisions" section

## Technical Tasks
1. Copy `docs/adr/template.md` to `docs/adr/004-multi-agent-coordination.md`.
2. Fill in each MADR section:
   - **Context:** The M4 requirement to coordinate 4 specialized agents on a research task
   - **Decision:** Use the LangGraph supervisor pattern with a central routing LLM
   - **Consequences / Positive:**
     - Clear single point of orchestration, easy to reason about
     - Supervisor state is explicit and inspectable in LangSmith
   - **Consequences / Negative:**
     - Supervisor is a bottleneck; all routing goes through one LLM call
     - Adding a new agent type requires updating the supervisor prompt
3. Add the comparison table:
   markdown
   | Pattern    | Routing        | Statefulness | Complexity | Research Fit |
   |------------|----------------|--------------|------------|--------------|
   | Supervisor | Central LLM    | High         | Medium     | High         |
   | Swarm      | Peer-to-peer   | Medium       | High       | Medium       |
   | Network    | Arbitrary      | Low          | Very High  | Low          |
   
4. Set `Status: Accepted` and `Date: 2026-03-28`.
5. Add a `## References` section linking to the LangGraph multi-agent docs and the M4 spike notes (`docs/planning/m4_multiagent_spike.md`).
6. Add a link in `README.md`:
   markdown
   ## Architecture Decisions
   - [ADR-004: Multi-agent coordination pattern](docs/adr/004-multi-agent-coordination.md)
   

## Context
MADR (Markdown Architecture Decision Records) are lightweight documents that capture the "why" behind architectural choices. In a Java enterprise project, this is equivalent to an RFC or design document in Confluence. The value is not the document itself but the discipline of thinking through trade-offs before committing to an approach. Future developers (including your future self) will thank you.

## References
- [MADR Format](https://adr.github.io/madr/) — the Markdown Architectural Decision Records specification
- [ADR Best Practices](https://cognitect.com/blog/2011/11/15/documenting-architecture-decisions) — Michael Nygard's original ADR proposal
