issues.title: Spike: React official tutorial + Next.js App Router basics
issues.description:
## User Story
As a Java engineer new to frontend development, I want to complete the official React and Next.js tutorials so that I understand component-based UI development well enough to build the DeepResearch web interface.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Complete the official React "Tic-Tac-Toe" tutorial and can explain what props, state, and components are
- [ ] Complete the Next.js App Router tutorial ("Next.js Learn") through the dashboard chapters
- [ ] Can explain the difference between Server Components and Client Components
- [ ] Can create a simple Next.js page with a TypeScript-typed React component that accepts props
- [ ] Can explain JSX syntax and how it compares to Java template engines (e.g., Thymeleaf)
- [ ] Write a short spike document (`docs/til/react-nextjs-basics.md`) summarizing key concepts and Java→React mappings

## Technical Tasks
1. **Work through the React tutorial** at https://react.dev/learn — focus on: components, props, state (`useState`), and event handlers.
2. **Work through the Next.js Learn course** at https://nextjs.org/learn — complete at minimum: App Router, layouts, pages, navigation, and data fetching chapters.
3. **Understand the Java→React concept mapping:**
   - Java class → React component (a function that returns UI)
   - Constructor arguments → props (read-only inputs to a component)
   - Instance fields → state (`useState` hook — mutable, triggers re-render)
   - Method → event handler (e.g., `onClick`, `onSubmit`)
   - Spring MVC Controller → Next.js page file (`app/page.tsx`)
   - JSP / Thymeleaf → JSX (HTML-like syntax embedded in TypeScript)
   - Spring Boot auto-scan → Next.js file-based routing (file path = URL)
   - Maven lifecycle → `npm run dev / build / start`
4. **Understand TypeScript basics for React:**
   - Typing props: `type Props = { query: string; onSubmit: (q: string) => void }`
   - Typing state: `const [value, setValue] = useState<string>("")`
5. **Understand Server vs Client Components:**
   - Server Components (default): rendered on server, can fetch data, cannot use `useState`/`useEffect`
   - Client Components: add `"use client"` directive at top of file, run in browser, can use hooks
6. **Write spike notes** in `docs/til/react-nextjs-basics.md` covering your key learnings and any Java analogies that helped.

## Context
This is a foundational spike before any UI code is written. React's mental model is fundamentally different from Java OOP: instead of mutable objects calling methods on each other, you build a tree of pure functions that return UI descriptions. Next.js App Router adds file-system routing, server-side rendering, and layout nesting on top of React — concepts that have no direct Java analog but are more approachable once React basics click. Investing time here prevents confusion during M5-3 through M5-12.

## References
- [React Official Tutorial (react.dev)](https://react.dev/learn/tutorial-tic-tac-toe) — interactive Tic-Tac-Toe tutorial; best first entry point for React
- [React "Thinking in React"](https://react.dev/learn/thinking-in-react) — mental model for decomposing UI into components
- [Next.js Learn Course](https://nextjs.org/learn) — official step-by-step App Router tutorial
- [Next.js App Router Docs](https://nextjs.org/docs/app) — reference for routing, layouts, Server/Client Components
- [TypeScript for Java Programmers](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-in-5-minutes-oop.html) — official TypeScript guide written specifically for OOP developers
- [MDN: JSX](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn_web/Tools_and_testing/Client-side_JavaScript_frameworks/React_getting_started#jsx) — JSX syntax explained

issues.title: Spike: Tailwind CSS + shadcn/ui component system
issues.description:
## User Story As a Java engineer new to CSS frameworks, I want to understand Tailwind CSS utility classes and the shadcn/ui component library so that I can style the DeepResearch UI consistently and efficiently without writing custom CSS from scratch. ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Can apply Tailwind utility classes to control layout (flex, grid), spacing, color, and typography without looking up every class - [ ] Can explain the difference between Tailwind (utility-first CSS) and traditional CSS frameworks like Bootstrap - [ ] Can add a shadcn/ui component to a Next.js page using the CLI (`npx shadcn@latest add `) - [ ] Build a small throwaway prototype page that uses at least: `Button`, `Input`, `Card`, and `ScrollArea` from shadcn/ui with Tailwind layout classes - [ ] Write a spike note (`docs/til/tailwind-shadcn.md`) documenting the components planned for M5-4 through M5-9 ## Technical Tasks 1. **Read the Tailwind CSS core concept docs** — focus on: utility-first fundamentals, responsive design (`sm:`, `md:`, `lg:` prefixes), flexbox utilities (`flex`, `items-center`, `gap-4`), and color/spacing scale. 2. **Practice Tailwind in the browser** using the Tailwind Play CDN sandbox at https://play.tailwindcss.com — try building a simple card layout. 3. **Understand the Tailwind mental model vs Java analogy:** - Traditional CSS = writing a named Java method for every behavior - Tailwind = composing behavior from small, single-purpose library methods inline - `className="flex items-center gap-4 p-6 bg-white rounded-lg shadow"` is like chaining builder methods 4. **Read the shadcn/ui introduction** at https://ui.shadcn.com/docs — understand that shadcn/ui is NOT an npm package; it copies component source code into your project so you own and can edit it. 5. **Add shadcn/ui to the spike project** (or use the project initialized in M5-3): bash npx shadcn@latest init npx shadcn@latest add button input card scroll-area 6. **Build a prototype layout** using the added components: tsx import { Button } from "@/components/ui/button" import { Input } from "@/components/ui/input" import { Card, CardContent, CardHeader, CardTitle } from "@/components/ui/card" export default function Prototype() { return (
Research Query
) } 7. **Document your component plan** in `docs/til/tailwind-shadcn.md`: list which shadcn/ui components you plan to use in each M5 issue (M5-4 through M5-9). ## Context Tailwind CSS eliminates the need to name CSS classes and context-switch between HTML and stylesheet files — a common pain point for developers coming from backend or Java-heavy stacks. shadcn/ui provides production-quality, accessible components (built on Radix UI primitives) that are fully customizable because the code lives in your project. Together they enable rapid, consistent UI development without design expertise. This spike prevents style-related churn during the feature issues that follow. ## References - [Tailwind CSS: Utility-First Fundamentals](https://tailwindcss.com/docs/utility-first) — the core mental model; read this first - [Tailwind CSS: Flexbox Utilities](https://tailwindcss.com/docs/flex) — layout reference used constantly in React UIs - [Tailwind CSS: Responsive Design](https://tailwindcss.com/docs/responsive-design) — breakpoint prefixes (`sm:`, `md:`, `lg:`) - [Tailwind Play (sandbox)](https://play.tailwindcss.com) — browser-based Tailwind playground, no install needed - [shadcn/ui: Introduction](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs) — how shadcn/ui works and why it copies code rather than packaging it - [shadcn/ui: Component List](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components) — full component reference - [Radix UI Primitives](https://www.radix-ui.com/primitives) — the accessible, headless primitives that shadcn/ui is built on

Style: **Default** - Base color: **Slate** - CSS variables: **Yes** 4. **Add the core components** needed across M5-4 through M5-9: bash npx shadcn@latest add button input card scroll-area textarea badge separator 5. **Replace `src/app/page.tsx`** with a typed placeholder: tsx export default function HomePage() { return (

)} ) } 3. **Install `@tailwindcss/typography`** for the `prose` class that styles Markdown content nicely: bash cd web && npm install @tailwindcss/typography Then add it to `tailwind.config.ts`: ts plugins: [require("@tailwindcss/typography")] 4. **Integrate into the home page:** After streaming completes, if `useResearch` exposes a `report` field, render `` below the chat. 5. **Add a print button:** tsx Add `@media print` CSS in `globals.css` to hide nav/sidebar during print: css @media print { .no-print { display: none; } } 6. **Test with a mock report object** — hardcode a `Report` with a Markdown body that contains `[1]`, `[2]` markers and a sources list, and verify rendering. ## Context The `prose` class from `@tailwindcss/typography` is a Tailwind plugin that applies a carefully designed typographic scale to raw HTML generated by Markdown parsers. issues.description:
## User Story As a user, I want a settings panel where I can select the AI model and configure which search API to use, so that I can tune the agent's behavior without modifying code. ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] A settings icon/button in the UI opens a settings dialog - [ ] The dialog contains a model selector (e.g., DeepSeek V3) and a search API selector (Tavily, Brave) - [ ] Selected settings are persisted to `localStorage` and survive a page refresh - [ ] Settings are sent as part of the research request payload to the backend - [ ] The dialog closes cleanly and does not submit a research query on close - [ ] No TypeScript errors (`npm run build` is clean) > **Note:** Model selection UI currently shows only DeepSeek V3. Additional models will be added when the model switching feature is implemented. ## Technical Tasks 1. **Add shadcn/ui Dialog and Select** if not yet added: ```bash npx shadcn@latest add dialog select ``` 2. **Create a `useSettings` hook** at `web/src/hooks/use-settings.ts` to manage settings with localStorage: ```ts "use client" import { useState, useEffect } from "react" export type Settings = { model: "deepseek-v3" searchApi: "tavily" | "brave" } const DEFAULTS: Settings = { model: "deepseek-v3", searchApi: "tavily" } const STORAGE_KEY = "deepresearch_settings" export function useSettings() { const [settings, setSettings] = useState(DEFAULTS) useEffect(() => { const stored = localStorage.getItem(STORAGE_KEY) if (stored) { try { setSettings(JSON.parse(stored)) } catch { /* ignore */ } } }, []) function updateSettings(next: Partial) { const merged = { ...settings, ...next } setSettings(merged) localStorage.setItem(STORAGE_KEY, JSON.stringify(merged)) } return { settings, updateSettings } } ``` 3. **Create `web/src/components/settings-dialog.tsx`:** ```tsx "use client" import { Settings as SettingsIcon } from "lucide-react" import { Button } from "@/components/ui/button" import { Dialog, DialogContent, DialogHeader, DialogTitle, DialogTrigger } from "@/components/ui/dialog" import { Select, SelectContent, SelectItem, SelectTrigger, SelectValue } from "@/components/ui/select" import { useSettings } from "@/hooks/use-settings" export function SettingsDialog() { const { settings, updateSettings } = useSettings() return ( Settings
) } ``` 4. **Add the settings button to the page header** and pass `settings` into `useResearch` so the hook includes them in the POST body: ```ts body: JSON.stringify({ query, model: settings.model, search_api: settings.searchApi }) ``` 5. **Update the FastAPI backend** to accept and respect `model` and `search_api` fields in the request body (if not already parameterized). 6. **Test persistence:** Change settings, refresh the page, and confirm selections are restored. ## Context `localStorage` is a browser key-value store that persists across page refreshes within the same origin — the equivalent of a simple properties file on the client side. The `useEffect` with an empty dependency array (`[]`) runs only once on mount, making it the right place to read initial values from storage — analogous to `@PostConstruct` in a Java Spring bean. The Dialog component from shadcn/ui is built on Radix UI's Dialog primitive, which handles focus trapping and accessibility automatically. ## References - [shadcn/ui: Dialog](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/dialog) — modal dialog component - [shadcn/ui: Select](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/select) — dropdown select component - [MDN: localStorage](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Window/localStorage) — browser storage API - [React: useEffect (on mount)](https://react.dev/reference/react/useEffect#connecting-to-an-external-system) — running code once on component mount

issues.title: Polish UI: loading states, error handling, responsive design
issues.description:
## User Story As a user, I want the UI to handle loading states gracefully, display clear error messages when things go wrong, and work well on both desktop and mobile screens, so that the application feels polished and trustworthy. ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] A skeleton loading state is shown in the message list while the first response is pending - [ ] A React Error Boundary wraps the main content area; unhandled errors display a user-friendly fallback UI instead of a blank page - [ ] Network errors from `useResearch` are displayed as a dismissible error banner - [ ] The layout is usable on a 375px-wide mobile screen (no horizontal overflow, touch targets ≥44px) - [ ] Dark mode works correctly: toggling OS dark mode applies the dark Tailwind theme throughout - [ ] `npm run build` is clean with no TypeScript or lint errors - [ ] Lighthouse accessibility score is ≥ 80 on the home page ## Technical Tasks 1. **Add a skeleton loading state.** Install shadcn/ui Skeleton if not yet added: bash npx shadcn@latest add skeleton In `MessageList`, show skeletons when `isLoading` is true and `messages` is empty: tsx if (isLoading && messages.length === 0) { return (
{[1, 2, 3].map((i) => )}
) } 2. **Create a React Error Boundary** at `web/src/components/error-boundary.tsx`. Error Boundaries must be class components in React: tsx "use client" import { Component, type ReactNode } from "react" import { Button } from "@/components/ui/button" type State = { hasError: boolean; message: string } export class ErrorBoundary extends Component<{ children: ReactNode }, State> { state: State = { hasError: false, message: "" } static getDerivedStateFromError(error: Error): State { return { hasError: true, message: error.message } } render() { if (this.state.hasError) { return (

Something went wrong: {this.state.message}

) } return this.props.children } } Wrap the main page content with ``. 3. **Add a dismissible error banner** in the home page for API errors from `useResearch`: tsx {error && (
{error}
)} Add a `clearError` function to the `useResearch` hook. 4. **Enable dark mode** in Tailwind. In `tailwind.config.ts`: ts darkMode: "class" shadcn/ui already supports dark mode via CSS variables. Apply `dark` class to `` based on `prefers-color-scheme` using Next.js `next-themes`: bash npm install next-themes Wrap the root layout body in ``. 5. **Check mobile layout:** Open Chrome DevTools, toggle the device toolbar to 375px width, and fix any overflow issues with Tailwind classes (`overflow-x-hidden`, `min-w-0`, `truncate`). 6. **Run a Lighthouse audit:** In Chrome DevTools → Lighthouse → run on the home page. Fix any accessibility issues flagged (common ones: missing `aria-label` on icon-only buttons, insufficient color contrast). 7. **Run `npm run build` and `npm run lint`** — fix all errors. ## Context Error Boundaries are one of the few places in modern React that still require a class component, because there is no Hook equivalent for `getDerivedStateFromError`. This is a known React limitation, not a reason to avoid Hooks elsewhere. Dark mode via `next-themes` reads the OS preference (`prefers-color-scheme`) and allows user override — the standard production pattern for Next.js apps. Lighthouse is Chrome's built-in audit tool; running it early surfaces accessibility issues before they accumulate. ## References - [React: Error Boundaries](https://react.dev/reference/react/Component#catching-rendering-errors-with-an-error-boundary) — official Error Boundary class component API - [next-themes: README](https://github.com/pacocoursey/next-themes) — dark mode provider for Next.js - [Tailwind CSS: Dark Mode](https://tailwindcss.com/docs/dark-mode) — class-based dark mode setup - [shadcn/ui: Dark Mode](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/dark-mode) — integrating dark mode with shadcn/ui - [shadcn/ui: Skeleton](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/skeleton) — loading skeleton component - [Google Lighthouse](https://developer.chrome.com/docs/lighthouse/overview/) — performance and accessibility auditing

issues.title: Deploy frontend to Vercel
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want the Next.js frontend deployed to Vercel so that users can access the DeepResearch UI via a public URL without running a local server.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The `/web` Next.js app is deployed to Vercel and accessible at a public URL (e.g., `deepresearch-agent.vercel.app`)
- [ ] The `NEXT_PUBLIC_BACKEND_URL` environment variable is set in Vercel pointing to the Railway backend URL (from M5-14)
- [ ] `npm run build` succeeds in the Vercel build log with no errors
- [ ] The deployed app successfully connects to the backend and streams a research response
- [ ] The Vercel project is linked to the GitHub repository and auto-deploys on push to `main`

## Technical Tasks
1. **Create a Vercel account** at https://vercel.com if you don't have one (free tier is sufficient).
2. **Install the Vercel CLI:**
   bash
   npm install -g vercel
   
3. **Log in and link the project:**
   bash
   cd /path/to/deepresearch-agent/web
   vercel login
   vercel link
   
   During `vercel link`, select "Create a new project" and set the root directory to `web/`.
4. **Configure the build settings** in the Vercel dashboard (or `vercel.json`):
   - Framework: **Next.js** (auto-detected)
   - Root Directory: `web`
   - Build command: `npm run build` (default)
   - Output directory: `.next` (default)
5. **Set environment variables** in the Vercel dashboard under Project → Settings → Environment Variables:
   
   NEXT_PUBLIC_BACKEND_URL = https://your-backend.railway.app
   
   (Set this to the Railway URL after completing M5-14. Use `http://localhost:8000` temporarily for initial deploy testing.)
6. **Deploy:**
   bash
   vercel --prod
   
7. **Configure GitHub integration:** In the Vercel dashboard, connect the GitHub repo under Project → Git. Set the production branch to `main`. Future pushes to `main` will auto-deploy.
8. **Verify the deployment:** Open the Vercel URL, submit a research query, and confirm end-to-end streaming works.

## Context
Vercel is the company that created Next.js, and their platform is the canonical deployment target for Next.js applications. Deployment is as simple as pointing Vercel at a GitHub repo — it detects Next.js automatically and configures the build pipeline. This is analogous to deploying a Spring Boot app to Heroku, but with zero configuration for a standard Next.js project. `NEXT_PUBLIC_` prefixed env vars are embedded at build time into the browser bundle — they are not secret and will be visible in the deployed JS.

## References
- [Vercel: Deploy a Next.js App](https://vercel.com/docs/frameworks/nextjs) — official Next.js deployment guide
- [Vercel: Environment Variables](https://vercel.com/docs/projects/environment-variables) — how to set and manage env vars
- [Vercel CLI Reference](https://vercel.com/docs/cli) — all CLI commands
- [Next.js: Deployment](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/deploying) — official Next.js deployment documentation

issues.title: Deploy backend API to Railway
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer, I want the FastAPI backend deployed to Railway so that it is accessible from the Vercel frontend and from the public internet.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The FastAPI backend is deployed to Railway and accessible at a public HTTPS URL
- [ ] The Railway service uses the existing `Dockerfile` (or `docker-compose.yml`) from the repo
- [ ] The `TAVILY_API_KEY`, `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY`, and other secrets are set as Railway environment variables (not committed to git)
- [ ] `GET /health` returns `{"status": "ok"}` at the public Railway URL
- [ ] CORS is configured to allow requests from the Vercel frontend URL
- [ ] The Vercel frontend (M5-13) successfully streams research results from the Railway backend

## Technical Tasks
1. **Create a Railway account** at https://railway.app (free trial available).
2. **Verify the `Dockerfile`** at the repo root is production-ready:
   dockerfile
   FROM python:3.12-slim
   WORKDIR /app
   COPY pyproject.toml uv.lock ./
   RUN pip install uv && uv sync --frozen --no-dev
   COPY src/ ./src/
   EXPOSE 8000
   CMD ["uv", "run", "uvicorn", "src.api:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
   
   If no `Dockerfile` exists, create one now.
3. **Add a health check endpoint** to `src/api.py` if not already present:
   python
   @app.get("/health")
   async def health():
       return {"status": "ok"}
   
4. **Deploy to Railway via CLI:**
   bash
   npm install -g @railway/cli
   railway login
   railway init          # Creates a new Railway project
   railway up            # Deploys using the Dockerfile
   
   Alternatively, use the Railway dashboard to connect the GitHub repo and enable auto-deploy.
5. **Set environment variables** in the Railway dashboard (Project → Variables):
   
   TAVILY_API_KEY=<your key>
   DEEPSEEK_API_KEY=<your key>
   LANGCHAIN_API_KEY=<your key>
   LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2=true
   
   Never commit these to git.
6. **Update CORS settings** in `src/api.py` to include the Vercel frontend URL:
   python
   allow_origins=[
       "http://localhost:3000",
       "https://your-frontend.vercel.app",
   ]
   
7. **Verify the deployment:**
   bash
   curl https://your-backend.railway.app/health
   # Expected: {"status": "ok"}
   
8. **Update `NEXT_PUBLIC_BACKEND_URL`** in Vercel environment variables to the Railway URL.

## Context
Railway is a modern PaaS (Platform as a Service) that deploys Docker containers with minimal configuration — similar to Heroku but with better Docker support. It reads the `Dockerfile` from your repo, builds the image, and runs it with a persistent URL. Environment variables set in Railway are injected at runtime (not build time), making it safe to store API keys there. This is equivalent to Spring Boot's `application-prod.properties` being injected as OS environment variables by the hosting platform.

## References
- [Railway: Deploy a Docker App](https://docs.railway.app/guides/dockerfiles) — deploying with a Dockerfile
- [Railway: Environment Variables](https://docs.railway.app/guides/variables) — setting and referencing env vars
- [Railway CLI Reference](https://docs.railway.app/guides/cli) — all CLI commands
- [FastAPI: Deployment with Docker](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/deployment/docker/) — official FastAPI Docker deployment guide
- [Docker: Python best practices](https://docs.docker.com/language/python/build-images/) — Dockerfile patterns for Python apps

## User Story
As a developer or potential user visiting the GitHub repository, I want a comprehensive README with screenshots, a live demo URL, and full setup instructions, so that I can understand the project and run it locally within 15 minutes.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The root `README.md` includes a demo URL (Vercel link) and at least 2 screenshots of the running UI
- [ ] Local setup instructions cover both backend and frontend and have been verified by following them on a clean environment
- [ ] The architecture section includes a Mermaid diagram showing the system components
- [ ] All environment variables needed for local development are documented (with placeholder values, not real keys)
- [ ] The README covers: what the project does, tech stack, prerequisites, local setup, running tests, and project structure
- [ ] No broken links

## Technical Tasks
1. **Take screenshots** of the running application:

   `markdown
   mermaid
   graph LR
       User -->|query| Frontend["Next.js Frontend\n(Vercel)"]
       Frontend -->|POST /api/research/stream| Backend["FastAPI Backend\n(Railway)"]

       Supervisor --> Analyst["Analyst Agent"]

4. **Document all environment variables** in a table:
   | Variable | Required | Description | Example |

   | `TAVILY_API_KEY` | Yes | Tavily search API key | `tvly-...` |
   | `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` | Yes | DeepSeek LLM API key | `...` |
   | `NEXT_PUBLIC_BACKEND_URL` | Yes (frontend) | Backend URL | `http://localhost:8000` |
5. **Verify setup instructions** end-to-end: follow the README on a clean terminal session and confirm the agent produces a research report.
6. **Check all links** — GitHub renders Mermaid diagrams natively, so verify the diagram renders correctly on GitHub.

## Context
A clear README is often the deciding factor in whether developers trust and adopt an open-source project. The Mermaid diagram renders natively on GitHub without any extra tooling — GitHub has supported Mermaid in Markdown since 2022. Screenshots can be captured with the OS screenshot tool or browser DevTools device mode.

## References
- [GitHub: Mermaid diagram support](https://github.blog/developer-skills/github/include-diagrams-in-your-markdown-files-with-mermaid/) — how Mermaid renders in GitHub Markdown

issues.title: Record demo video or create screenshot walkthrough
issues.description:
## User Story
As a developer sharing the project publicly, I want a demo video or screenshot walkthrough that shows the agent working end-to-end, so that visitors can understand the product's value without running it locally.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A demo video (≤5 minutes) OR a GIF/screenshot walkthrough covering the full research workflow exists and is linked from the README
- [ ] The walkthrough shows: entering a query → streaming response → progress panel → final report with citations
- [ ] The demo uses a realistic, interesting research question (not "hello world")
- [ ] The demo artifact is either hosted (YouTube, Loom, GitHub releases) or committed as a GIF (≤10MB) to `docs/demo/`
- [ ] The README `## Demo` section is updated with the link or embedded GIF

## Technical Tasks
1. **Choose a format:**
   - **GIF:** Lightweight, embeds directly in GitHub README. Use [Kap](https://getkap.co/) (macOS) or [ScreenToGif](https://www.screentogif.com/) (Windows) to record and export. Keep under 10MB by reducing frame rate (10fps) or duration.
   - **Video:** Loom (https://www.loom.com) or YouTube (unlisted). Record with OBS or QuickTime. 3–5 minutes is ideal.
2. **Prepare the demo script** before recording:
   
   1. Open the app in browser
   2. Type: "What are the key differences between LangGraph and AutoGen for building multi-agent systems?"
   3. Show the streaming response appearing token-by-token
   4. Highlight the research progress panel (plan steps checking off)
   5. Scroll through the final report and click a citation link
   6. (Optional) Open the history sidebar and revisit the session
   
3. **Record the demo** with a clean browser (close unrelated tabs, use incognito if needed). Ensure the backend is running and connected before recording.
4. **Optimize the artifact:**
   - If GIF: target ≤10MB. Use [ezgif.com](https://ezgif.com) to optimize if needed.
   - If video: ensure audio narration (optional but recommended) explains what the agent is doing.
5. **Store and link the artifact:**
   - GIF: save to `docs/demo/walkthrough.gif` and embed in README: `![Demo](docs/demo/walkthrough.gif)`
   - Video: add the URL to README: `[Watch the demo on Loom](https://loom.com/share/...)`
6. **Update the README** `## Demo` section with the artifact.

## Context
A visual demo dramatically lowers the barrier for evaluating a project. For an AI agent that streams responses, a GIF or video is far more compelling than static screenshots because it shows the real-time behavior. Loom is a free, popular tool for quick screen recordings with instant shareable links — no video editing experience needed. This is the final milestone deliverable and the capstone of the M5 milestone.
